tree.name: Comprehensive Categorization for Software Assurance Trends

children.name: Access Control
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to access control.

children.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Weak Access Permissions for EJB Methods
children.description: If elevated access rights are assigned to EJB methods, then an attacker can take advantage of the permissions to exploit the product.

children.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Password in Configuration File
children.description: Storing a plaintext password in a configuration file allows anyone who can read the file access to the password-protected resource making them an easy target for attackers.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information Through Data Queries
children.description: When trying to keep information confidential, an attacker can often infer some of the information by using statistics.

children.name: Plaintext Storage of a Password
children.description: The product stores a password in plaintext within resources such as memory or files.

children.name: Storing Passwords in a Recoverable Format
children.description: The storage of passwords in a recoverable format makes them subject to password reuse attacks by malicious users. In fact, it should be noted that recoverable encrypted passwords provide no significant benefit over plaintext passwords since they are subject not only to reuse by malicious attackers b

children.name: Empty Password in Configuration File
children.description: Using an empty string as a password is insecure.

children.name: Use of Hard-coded Password
children.description: The product contains a hard-coded password, which it uses for its own inbound authentication or for outbound communication to external components.

children.name: Password in Configuration File
children.description: The product stores a password in a configuration file that might be accessible to actors who do not know the password.

children.name: Weak Encoding for Password
children.description: Obscuring a password with a trivial encoding does not protect the password.

children.name: Not Using Password Aging
children.description: The product does not have a mechanism in place for managing password aging.

children.name: Password Aging with Long Expiration
children.description: The product supports password aging, but the expiration period is too long.

children.name: Incorrect Privilege Assignment
children.description: A product incorrectly assigns a privilege to a particular actor, creating an unintended sphere of control for that actor.

children.name: Privilege Defined With Unsafe Actions
children.description: A particular privilege, role, capability, or right can be used to perform unsafe actions that were not intended, even when it is assigned to the correct entity.

children.name: Privilege Chaining
children.description: Two distinct privileges, roles, capabilities, or rights can be combined in a way that allows an entity to perform unsafe actions that would not be allowed without that combination.

children.name: Improper Privilege Management
children.description: The product does not properly assign, modify, track, or check privileges for an actor, creating an unintended sphere of control for that actor.

children.name: Privilege Context Switching Error
children.description: The product does not properly manage privileges while it is switching between different contexts that have different privileges or spheres of control.

children.name: Privilege Dropping / Lowering Errors
children.description: The product does not drop privileges before passing control of a resource to an actor that does not have those privileges.

children.name: Least Privilege Violation
children.description: The elevated privilege level required to perform operations such as chroot() should be dropped immediately after the operation is performed.

children.name: Improper Check for Dropped Privileges
children.description: The product attempts to drop privileges but does not check or incorrectly checks to see if the drop succeeded.

children.name: Improper Handling of Insufficient Privileges
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when it has insufficient privileges to perform an operation, leading to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Incorrect Default Permissions
children.description: During installation, installed file permissions are set to allow anyone to modify those files.

children.name: Insecure Inherited Permissions
children.description: A product defines a set of insecure permissions that are inherited by objects that are created by the program.

children.name: Insecure Preserved Inherited Permissions
children.description: A product inherits a set of insecure permissions for an object, e.g. when copying from an archive file, without user awareness or involvement.

children.name: Incorrect Execution-Assigned Permissions
children.description: While it is executing, the product sets the permissions of an object in a way that violates the intended permissions that have been specified by the user.

children.name: Improper Handling of Insufficient Permissions or Privileges
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when it has insufficient privileges to access resources or functionality as specified by their permissions. This may cause it to follow unexpected code paths that may leave the product in an invalid state.

children.name: Improper Preservation of Permissions
children.description: The product does not preserve permissions or incorrectly preserves permissions when copying, restoring, or sharing objects, which can cause them to have less restrictive permissions than intended.

children.name: Improper Ownership Management
children.description: The product assigns the wrong ownership, or does not properly verify the ownership, of an object or resource.

children.name: Unverified Ownership
children.description: The product does not properly verify that a critical resource is owned by the proper entity.

children.name: Improper Access Control
children.description: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access to a resource from an unauthorized actor.

children.name: Improper Authorization
children.description: The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.

children.name: Incorrect User Management
children.description: The product does not properly manage a user within its environment.

children.name: Improper Authentication
children.description: When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.

children.name: Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel
children.description: The product requires authentication, but the product has an alternate path or channel that does not require authentication.

children.name: Authentication Bypass by Alternate Name
children.description: The product performs authentication based on the name of a resource being accessed, or the name of the actor performing the access, but it does not properly check all possible names for that resource or actor.

children.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
children.description: This attack-focused weakness is caused by incorrectly implemented authentication schemes that are subject to spoofing attacks.

children.name: Reliance on IP Address for Authentication
children.description: The product uses an IP address for authentication.

children.name: Using Referer Field for Authentication
children.description: The referer field in HTTP requests can be easily modified and, as such, is not a valid means of message integrity checking.

children.name: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
children.description: A capture-replay flaw exists when the design of the product makes it possible for a malicious user to sniff network traffic and bypass authentication by replaying it to the server in question to the same effect as the original message (or with minor changes).

children.name: Improper Certificate Validation
children.description: The product does not validate, or incorrectly validates, a certificate.

children.name: Improper Following of a Certificate's Chain of Trust
children.description: The product does not follow, or incorrectly follows, the chain of trust for a certificate back to a trusted root certificate, resulting in incorrect trust of any resource that is associated with that certificate.

children.name: Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch
children.description: The product communicates with a host that provides a certificate, but the product does not properly ensure that the certificate is actually associated with that host.

children.name: Improper Validation of Certificate Expiration
children.description: A certificate expiration is not validated or is incorrectly validated, so trust may be assigned to certificates that have been abandoned due to age.

children.name: Improper Check for Certificate Revocation
children.description: The product does not check or incorrectly checks the revocation status of a certificate, which may cause it to use a certificate that has been compromised.

children.name: Channel Accessible by Non-Endpoint
children.description: The product does not adequately verify the identity of actors at both ends of a communication channel, or does not adequately ensure the integrity of the channel, in a way that allows the channel to be accessed or influenced by an actor that is not an endpoint.

children.name: Reflection Attack in an Authentication Protocol
children.description: Simple authentication protocols are subject to reflection attacks if a malicious user can use the target machine to impersonate a trusted user.

children.name: Authentication Bypass by Assumed-Immutable Data
children.description: The authentication scheme or implementation uses key data elements that are assumed to be immutable, but can be controlled or modified by the attacker.

children.name: Incorrect Implementation of Authentication Algorithm
children.description: The requirements for the product dictate the use of an established authentication algorithm, but the implementation of the algorithm is incorrect.

children.name: Missing Critical Step in Authentication
children.description: The product implements an authentication technique, but it skips a step that weakens the technique.

children.name: Authentication Bypass by Primary Weakness
children.description: The authentication algorithm is sound, but the implemented mechanism can be bypassed as the result of a separate weakness that is primary to the authentication error.

children.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
children.description: The product does not perform any authentication for functionality that requires a provable user identity or consumes a significant amount of resources.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
children.description: The product does not implement sufficient measures to prevent multiple failed authentication attempts within a short time frame.

children.name: Use of Single-factor Authentication
children.description: The product uses an authentication algorithm that uses a single factor (e.g., a password) in a security context that should require more than one factor.

children.name: Use of Password System for Primary Authentication
children.description: The use of password systems as the primary means of authentication may be subject to several flaws or shortcomings, each reducing the effectiveness of the mechanism.

children.name: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key
children.description: The product uses a hard-coded, unchangeable cryptographic key.

children.name: Key Exchange without Entity Authentication
children.description: The product performs a key exchange with an actor without verifying the identity of that actor.

children.name: Reliance on Reverse DNS Resolution for a Security-Critical Action
children.description: The product performs reverse DNS resolution on an IP address to obtain the hostname and make a security decision, but it does not properly ensure that the IP address is truly associated with the hostname.

children.name: Missing Check for Certificate Revocation after Initial Check
children.description: The product does not check the revocation status of a certificate after its initial revocation check, which can cause the product to perform privileged actions even after the certificate is revoked at a later time.

children.name: Session Fixation
children.description: Authenticating a user, or otherwise establishing a new user session, without invalidating any existing session identifier gives an attacker the opportunity to steal authenticated sessions.

children.name: Unprotected Primary Channel
children.description: The product uses a primary channel for administration or restricted functionality, but it does not properly protect the channel.

children.name: Unprotected Alternate Channel
children.description: The product protects a primary channel, but it does not use the same level of protection for an alternate channel.

children.name: Race Condition During Access to Alternate Channel
children.description: The product opens an alternate channel to communicate with an authorized user, but the channel is accessible to other actors.

children.name: Unprotected Windows Messaging Channel ('Shatter')
children.description: The product does not properly verify the source of a message in the Windows Messaging System while running at elevated privileges, creating an alternate channel through which an attacker can directly send a message to the product.

children.name: Direct Request ('Forced Browsing')
children.description: The web application does not adequately enforce appropriate authorization on all restricted URLs, scripts, or files.

children.name: Unintended Proxy or Intermediary ('Confused Deputy')
children.description: The product receives a request, message, or directive from an upstream component, but the product does not sufficiently preserve the original source of the request before forwarding the request to an external actor that is outside of the product's control sphere. This causes the product to appear to

children.name: .NET Misconfiguration: Use of Impersonation
children.description: Allowing a .NET application to run at potentially escalated levels of access to the underlying operating and file systems can be dangerous and result in various forms of attacks.

children.name: Weak Password Requirements
children.description: The product does not require that users should have strong passwords.

children.name: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
children.description: The product transmits or stores authentication credentials, but it uses an insecure method that is susceptible to unauthorized interception and/or retrieval.

children.name: Unprotected Transport of Credentials
children.description: Login pages do not use adequate measures to protect the user name and password while they are in transit from the client to the server.

children.name: Missing Password Field Masking
children.description: The product does not mask passwords during entry, increasing the potential for attackers to observe and capture passwords.

children.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Authorization Before Parsing and Canonicalization
children.description: If a web server does not fully parse requested URLs before it examines them for authorization, it may be possible for an attacker to bypass authorization protection.

children.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Plaintext Password in Configuration File
children.description: The J2EE application stores a plaintext password in a configuration file.

children.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Use of Identity Impersonation
children.description: Configuring an ASP.NET application to run with impersonated credentials may give the application unnecessary privileges.

children.name: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled SQL Primary Key
children.description: The product uses a database table that includes records that should not be accessible to an actor, but it executes a SQL statement with a primary key that can be controlled by that actor.

children.name: Authentication Bypass: OpenSSL CTX Object Modified after SSL Objects are Created
children.description: The product modifies the SSL context after connection creation has begun.

children.name: Missing Validation of OpenSSL Certificate
children.description: The product uses OpenSSL and trusts or uses a certificate without using the SSL_get_verify_result() function to ensure that the certificate satisfies all necessary security requirements.

children.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
children.description: The web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a redirect.

children.name: Use of Client-Side Authentication
children.description: A client/server product performs authentication within client code but not in server code, allowing server-side authentication to be bypassed via a modified client that omits the authentication check.

children.name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
children.description: The product processes an XML document that can contain XML entities with URIs that resolve to documents outside of the intended sphere of control, causing the product to embed incorrect documents into its output.

children.name: Improper Authorization of Index Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The product creates a search index of private or sensitive documents, but it does not properly limit index access to actors who are authorized to see the original information.

children.name: Insufficient Session Expiration
children.description: According to WASC, "Insufficient Session Expiration is when a web site permits an attacker to reuse old session credentials or session IDs for authorization."

children.name: Unverified Password Change
children.description: When setting a new password for a user, the product does not require knowledge of the original password, or using another form of authentication.

children.name: Unsafe ActiveX Control Marked Safe For Scripting
children.description: An ActiveX control is intended for restricted use, but it has been marked as safe-for-scripting.

children.name: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
children.description: The system's authorization functionality does not prevent one user from gaining access to another user's data or record by modifying the key value identifying the data.

children.name: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
children.description: The product contains a mechanism for users to recover or change their passwords without knowing the original password, but the mechanism is weak.

children.name: Overly Restrictive Account Lockout Mechanism
children.description: The product contains an account lockout protection mechanism, but the mechanism is too restrictive and can be triggered too easily, which allows attackers to deny service to legitimate users by causing their accounts to be locked out.

children.name: Use of Non-Canonical URL Paths for Authorization Decisions
children.description: The product defines policy namespaces and makes authorization decisions based on the assumption that a URL is canonical. This can allow a non-canonical URL to bypass the authorization.

children.name: Incorrect Use of Privileged APIs
children.description: The product does not conform to the API requirements for a function call that requires extra privileges. This could allow attackers to gain privileges by causing the function to be called incorrectly.

children.name: Incorrect Ownership Assignment
children.description: The product assigns an owner to a resource, but the owner is outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
children.description: The product specifies permissions for a security-critical resource in a way that allows that resource to be read or modified by unintended actors.

children.name: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
children.description: The product contains hard-coded credentials, such as a password or cryptographic key.

children.name: Guessable CAPTCHA
children.description: The product uses a CAPTCHA challenge, but the challenge can be guessed or automatically recognized by a non-human actor.

children.name: Use of Password Hash Instead of Password for Authentication
children.description: The product records password hashes in a data store, receives a hash of a password from a client, and compares the supplied hash to the hash obtained from the data store.

children.name: Placement of User into Incorrect Group
children.description: The product or the administrator places a user into an incorrect group.

children.name: Missing Authorization
children.description: The product does not perform an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.

children.name: Incorrect Authorization
children.description: The product performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action, but it does not correctly perform the check.

children.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
children.description: The web server receives a URL or similar request from an upstream component and retrieves the contents of this URL, but it does not sufficiently ensure that the request is being sent to the expected destination.

children.name: Storage of Sensitive Data in a Mechanism without Access Control
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in a file system or device that does not have built-in access control.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Communication Channel to Intended Endpoints
children.description: The product establishes a communication channel to (or from) an endpoint for privileged or protected operations, but it does not properly ensure that it is communicating with the correct endpoint.

children.name: Improper Verification of Intent by Broadcast Receiver
children.description: The Android application uses a Broadcast Receiver that receives an Intent but does not properly verify that the Intent came from an authorized source.

children.name: Improper Export of Android Application Components
children.description: The Android application exports a component for use by other applications, but does not properly restrict which applications can launch the component or access the data it contains.

children.name: Use of Implicit Intent for Sensitive Communication
children.description: The Android application uses an implicit intent for transmitting sensitive data to other applications.

children.name: Improper Authorization in Handler for Custom URL Scheme
children.description: The product uses a handler for a custom URL scheme, but it does not properly restrict which actors can invoke the handler using the scheme.

children.name: Improper Verification of Source of a Communication Channel
children.description: The product establishes a communication channel to handle an incoming request that has been initiated by an actor, but it does not properly verify that the request is coming from the expected origin.

children.name: Incorrectly Specified Destination in a Communication Channel
children.description: The product creates a communication channel to initiate an outgoing request to an actor, but it does not correctly specify the intended destination for that actor.

children.name: Permissive Cross-domain Security Policy with Untrusted Domains
children.description:
The product uses a web-client protection
         mechanism such as a Content Security Policy (CSP) or
         cross-domain policy file, but the policy includes untrusted
         domains with which the web client is allowed to
         communicate.

children.name: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag
children.description: The product uses a cookie to store sensitive information, but the cookie is not marked with the HttpOnly flag.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames
children.description: The web application does not restrict or incorrectly restricts frame objects or UI layers that belong to another application or domain, which can lead to user confusion about which interface the user is interacting with.

children.name: Use of Web Link to Untrusted Target with window.opener Access
children.description: The web application produces links to untrusted external sites outside of its sphere of control, but it does not properly prevent the external site from modifying security-critical properties of the window.opener object, such as the location property.

children.name: On-Chip Debug and Test Interface With Improper Access Control
children.description: The chip does not implement or does not correctly perform access control to check whether users are authorized to access internal registers and test modes through the physical debug/test interface.

children.name: Insufficient Granularity of Access Control
children.description: The product implements access controls via a policy or other feature with the intention to disable or restrict accesses (reads and/or writes) to assets in a system from untrusted agents. However, implemented access controls lack required granularity, which renders the control policy too broad becaus

children.name: Insufficient Granularity of Address Regions Protected by Register Locks
children.description: The product defines a large address region protected from modification by the same register lock control bit. This results in a conflict between the functional requirement that some addresses need to be writable by software during operation and the security requirement that the system configuration

children.name: Improper Restriction of Write-Once Bit Fields
children.description: The hardware design control register "sticky bits" or write-once bit fields are improperly implemented, such that they can be reprogrammed by software.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information Through Metadata
children.description: The product prevents direct access to a resource containing sensitive information, but it does not sufficiently limit access to metadata that is derived from the original, sensitive information.

children.name: Improper Prevention of Lock Bit Modification
children.description: The product uses a trusted lock bit for restricting access to registers, address regions, or other resources, but the product does not prevent the value of the lock bit from being modified after it has been set.

children.name: Security-Sensitive Hardware Controls with Missing Lock Bit Protection
children.description: The product uses a register lock bit protection mechanism, but it does not ensure that the lock bit prevents modification of system registers or controls that perform changes to important hardware system configuration.

children.name: Inclusion of Undocumented Features or Chicken Bits
children.description: The device includes chicken bits or undocumented features that can create entry points for unauthorized actors.

children.name: Sensitive Non-Volatile Information Not Protected During Debug
children.description: Access to security-sensitive information stored in fuses is not limited during debug.

children.name: Internal Asset Exposed to Unsafe Debug Access Level or State
children.description:
The product uses physical debug or test
        interfaces with support for multiple access levels, but it
        assigns the wrong debug access level to an internal asset,
        providing unintended access to the asset from untrusted debug
        agents.

children.name: CPU Hardware Not Configured to Support Exclusivity of Write and Execute Operations
children.description: The CPU is not configured to provide hardware support for exclusivity of write and execute operations on memory. This allows an attacker to execute data from all of memory.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Software Interfaces to Hardware Features
children.description:
The product provides software-controllable
			device functionality for capabilities such as power and
			clock management, but it does not properly limit
			functionality that can lead to modification of
			hardware memory or register bits, or the ability to
			observe physical side channels.

children.name: Improper Access Control Applied to Mirrored or Aliased Memory Regions
children.description: Aliased or mirrored memory regions in hardware designs may have inconsistent read/write permissions enforced by the hardware. A possible result is that an untrusted agent is blocked from accessing a memory region but is not blocked from accessing the corresponding aliased memory region.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Security Token Assignment
children.description: The System-On-A-Chip (SoC) implements a Security Token mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. However, the Security Tokens are improperly protected.

children.name: Improper Handling of Overlap Between Protected Memory Ranges
children.description: The product allows address regions to overlap, which can result in the bypassing of intended memory protection.

children.name: Improper Access Control for Register Interface
children.description: The product uses memory-mapped I/O registers that act as an interface to hardware functionality from software, but there is improper access control to those registers.

children.name: Improper Physical Access Control
children.description: The product is designed with access restricted to certain information, but it does not sufficiently protect against an unauthorized actor with physical access to these areas.

children.name: Policy Uses Obsolete Encoding
children.description: The product uses an obsolete encoding mechanism to implement access controls.

children.name: Policy Privileges are not Assigned Consistently Between Control and Data Agents
children.description: The product's hardware-enforced access control for a particular resource improperly accounts for privilege discrepancies between control and write policies.

children.name: Generation of Incorrect Security Tokens
children.description: The product implements a Security Token mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. However, the Security Tokens generated in the system are incorrect.

children.name: Improper Access Control for Volatile Memory Containing Boot Code
children.description: The product conducts a secure-boot process that transfers bootloader code from Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) into Volatile Memory (VM), but it does not have sufficient access control or other protections for the Volatile Memory.

children.name: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
children.description: The SameSite attribute for sensitive cookies is not set, or an insecure value is used.

children.name: Hardware Child Block Incorrectly Connected to Parent System
children.description: Signals between a hardware IP and the parent system design are incorrectly connected causing security risks.

children.name: Mutable Attestation or Measurement Reporting Data
children.description: The register contents used for attestation or measurement reporting data to verify boot flow are modifiable by an adversary.

children.name: Incorrect Decoding of Security Identifiers
children.description: The product implements a decoding mechanism to decode certain bus-transaction signals to security identifiers. If the decoding is implemented incorrectly, then untrusted agents can now gain unauthorized access to the asset.

children.name: Incorrect Conversion of Security Identifiers
children.description: The product implements a conversion mechanism to map certain bus-transaction signals to security identifiers. However, if the conversion is incorrectly implemented, untrusted agents can gain unauthorized access to the asset.

children.name: Insecure Security Identifier Mechanism
children.description: The System-on-Chip (SoC) implements a Security Identifier mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. However, the Security Identifiers are not correctly implemented.

children.name: Incorrect Chaining or Granularity of Debug Components
children.description: The product's debug components contain incorrect chaining or granularity of debug components.

children.name: Unprotected Confidential Information on Device is Accessible by OSAT Vendors
children.description: The product does not adequately protect confidential information on the device from being accessed by Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) vendors.

children.name: Missing Protection Mechanism for Alternate Hardware Interface
children.description:
The lack of protections on alternate paths to access
                control-protected assets (such as unprotected shadow registers
                and other external facing unguarded interfaces) allows an
                attacker to bypass existing protections to the asset that are
		only performed

children.name: Missing Source Identifier in Entity Transactions on a System-On-Chip (SOC)
children.description: The product implements a security identifier mechanism to differentiate what actions are allowed or disallowed when a transaction originates from an entity. A transaction is sent without a security identifier.

children.name: Improperly Preserved Integrity of Hardware Configuration State During a Power Save/Restore Operation
children.description:
The product performs a power save/restore
            operation, but it does not ensure that the integrity of
            the configuration state is maintained and/or verified between
	    the beginning and ending of the operation.

children.name: Improper Translation of Security Attributes by Fabric Bridge
children.description: The bridge incorrectly translates security attributes from either trusted to untrusted or from untrusted to trusted when converting from one fabric protocol to another.

children.name: Missing Protection for Mirrored Regions in On-Chip Fabric Firewall
children.description: The firewall in an on-chip fabric protects the main addressed region, but it does not protect any mirrored memory or memory-mapped-IO (MMIO) regions.

children.name: Hardware Allows Activation of Test or Debug Logic at Runtime
children.description: During runtime, the hardware allows for test or debug logic (feature) to be activated, which allows for changing the state of the hardware. This feature can alter the intended behavior of the system and allow for alteration and leakage of sensitive data by an adversary.

children.name: Missing Write Protection for Parametric Data Values
children.description: The device does not write-protect the parametric data values for sensors that scale the sensor value, allowing untrusted software to manipulate the apparent result and potentially damage hardware or cause operational failure.

children.name: Improper Setting of Bus Controlling Capability in Fabric End-point
children.description: The bus controller enables bits in the fabric end-point to allow responder devices to control transactions on the fabric.

children.name: Fabric-Address Map Allows Programming of Unwarranted Overlaps of Protected and Unprotected Ranges
children.description: The address map of the on-chip fabric has protected and unprotected regions overlapping, allowing an attacker to bypass access control to the overlapping portion of the protected region.

children.name: Improper Access Control in Fabric Bridge
children.description: The product uses a fabric bridge for transactions between two Intellectual Property (IP) blocks, but the bridge does not properly perform the expected privilege, identity, or other access control checks between those IP blocks.

children.name: Improper Protection for Outbound Error Messages and Alert Signals
children.description: Untrusted agents can disable alerts about signal conditions exceeding limits or the response mechanism that handles such alerts.

children.name: Improper Management of Sensitive Trace Data
children.description:
Trace data collected from several sources on the
                System-on-Chip (SoC) is stored in unprotected locations or
                transported to untrusted agents.

children.name: Security Version Number Mutable to Older Versions
children.description: Security-version number in hardware is mutable, resulting in the ability to downgrade (roll-back) the boot firmware to vulnerable code versions.

children.name: Unauthorized Error Injection Can Degrade Hardware Redundancy
children.description: An unauthorized agent can inject errors into a redundant block to deprive the system of redundancy or put the system in a degraded operating mode.

children.name: Weak Authentication
children.description: The product uses an authentication mechanism to restrict access to specific users or identities, but the mechanism does not sufficiently prove that the claimed identity is correct.

children.name: Use of Weak Credentials
children.description: The product uses weak credentials (such as a default key or hard-coded password) that can be calculated, derived, reused, or guessed by an attacker.

children.name: Use of Default Credentials
children.description: The product uses default credentials (such as passwords or cryptographic keys) for potentially critical functionality.

children.name: Use of Default Password
children.description: The product uses default passwords for potentially critical functionality.

children.name: Use of Default Cryptographic Key
children.description: The product uses a default cryptographic key for potentially critical functionality.

children.name: Comparison
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to comparison.

children.name: Permissive List of Allowed Inputs
children.description: The product implements a protection mechanism that relies on a list of inputs (or properties of inputs) that are explicitly allowed by policy because the inputs are assumed to be safe, but the list is too permissive - that is, it allows an input that is unsafe, leading to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Incorrect Regular Expression
children.description: The product specifies a regular expression in a way that causes data to be improperly matched or compared.

children.name: Overly Restrictive Regular Expression
children.description: A regular expression is overly restrictive, which prevents dangerous values from being detected.

children.name: Partial String Comparison
children.description: The product performs a comparison that only examines a portion of a factor before determining whether there is a match, such as a substring, leading to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Missing Default Case in Multiple Condition Expression
children.description: The code does not have a default case in an expression with multiple conditions, such as a switch statement.

children.name: Comparison of Classes by Name
children.description: The product compares classes by name, which can cause it to use the wrong class when multiple classes can have the same name.

children.name: Comparison of Object References Instead of Object Contents
children.description: The product compares object references instead of the contents of the objects themselves, preventing it from detecting equivalent objects.

children.name: Use of Wrong Operator in String Comparison
children.description: The product uses the wrong operator when comparing a string, such as using "==" when the .equals() method should be used instead.

children.name: Permissive Regular Expression
children.description: The product uses a regular expression that does not sufficiently restrict the set of allowed values.

children.name: Incorrect Comparison
children.description: The product compares two entities in a security-relevant context, but the comparison is incorrect.

children.name: Regular Expression without Anchors
children.description: The product uses a regular expression to perform neutralization, but the regular expression is not anchored and may allow malicious or malformed data to slip through.

children.name: Numeric Range Comparison Without Minimum Check
children.description: The product checks a value to ensure that it is less than or equal to a maximum, but it does not also verify that the value is greater than or equal to the minimum.

children.name: Incomplete Comparison with Missing Factors
children.description: The product performs a comparison between entities that must consider multiple factors or characteristics of each entity, but the comparison does not include one or more of these factors.

children.name: Comparison of Incompatible Types
children.description: The product performs a comparison between two entities, but the entities are of different, incompatible types that cannot be guaranteed to provide correct results when they are directly compared.

children.name: Comparison Using Wrong Factors
children.description: The code performs a comparison between two entities, but the comparison examines the wrong factors or characteristics of the entities, which can lead to incorrect results and resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Floating Point Comparison with Incorrect Operator
children.description:
The code performs a comparison such as an
        equality test between two float (floating point) values, but
        it uses comparison operators that do not account for the
        possibility of loss of precision.

children.name: Component Interaction
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to component interaction.

children.name: Compiler Removal of Code to Clear Buffers
children.description: Sensitive memory is cleared according to the source code, but compiler optimizations leave the memory untouched when it is not read from again, aka "dead store removal."

children.name: Misinterpretation of Input
children.description: The product misinterprets an input, whether from an attacker or another product, in a security-relevant fashion.

children.name: Improper Interaction Between Multiple Correctly-Behaving Entities
children.description: An interaction error occurs when two entities have correct behavior when running independently of each other, but when they are integrated as components in a larger system or process, they introduce incorrect behaviors that may cause resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Interpretation Conflict
children.description: Product A handles inputs or steps differently than Product B, which causes A to perform incorrect actions based on its perception of B's state.

children.name: Incomplete Model of Endpoint Features
children.description: A product acts as an intermediary or monitor between two or more endpoints, but it does not have a complete model of an endpoint's features, behaviors, or state, potentially causing the product to perform incorrect actions based on this incomplete model.

children.name: Behavioral Change in New Version or Environment
children.description: A's behavior or functionality changes with a new version of A, or a new environment, which is not known (or manageable) by B.

children.name: Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests ('HTTP Request/Response Smuggling')
children.description:
The product acts as an intermediary HTTP agent
         (such as a proxy or firewall) in the data flow between two
         entities such as a client and server, but it does not
         interpret malformed HTTP requests or responses in ways that
         are consistent with how the messages will be

children.name: Trusting HTTP Permission Methods on the Server Side
children.description: The server contains a protection mechanism that assumes that any URI that is accessed using HTTP GET will not cause a state change to the associated resource. This might allow attackers to bypass intended access restrictions and conduct resource modification and deletion attacks, since some applicat

children.name: Compiler Optimization Removal or Modification of Security-critical Code
children.description: The developer builds a security-critical protection mechanism into the software, but the compiler optimizes the program such that the mechanism is removed or modified.

children.name: Processor Optimization Removal or Modification of Security-critical Code
children.description: The developer builds a security-critical protection mechanism into the software, but the processor optimizes the execution of the program such that the mechanism is removed or modified.

children.name: Insecure Automated Optimizations
children.description: The product uses a mechanism that automatically optimizes code, e.g. to improve a characteristic such as performance, but the optimizations can have an unintended side effect that might violate an intended security assumption.

children.name: Concurrency
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to concurrency.

children.name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
children.description: The product contains a concurrent code sequence that requires temporary, exclusive access to a shared resource, but a timing window exists in which the shared resource can be modified by another code sequence operating concurrently.

children.name: Race Condition Enabling Link Following
children.description: The product checks the status of a file or directory before accessing it, which produces a race condition in which the file can be replaced with a link before the access is performed, causing the product to access the wrong file.

children.name: Signal Handler Race Condition
children.description: The product uses a signal handler that introduces a race condition.

children.name: Race Condition within a Thread
children.description: If two threads of execution use a resource simultaneously, there exists the possibility that resources may be used while invalid, in turn making the state of execution undefined.

children.name: Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race Condition
children.description: The product checks the state of a resource before using that resource, but the resource's state can change between the check and the use in a way that invalidates the results of the check.

children.name: Context Switching Race Condition
children.description: A product performs a series of non-atomic actions to switch between contexts that cross privilege or other security boundaries, but a race condition allows an attacker to modify or misrepresent the product's behavior during the switch.

children.name: Unrestricted Externally Accessible Lock
children.description: The product properly checks for the existence of a lock, but the lock can be externally controlled or influenced by an actor that is outside of the intended sphere of control.

children.name: Improper Resource Locking
children.description: The product does not lock or does not correctly lock a resource when the product must have exclusive access to the resource.

children.name: Missing Lock Check
children.description: A product does not check to see if a lock is present before performing sensitive operations on a resource.

children.name: Dangerous Signal Handler not Disabled During Sensitive Operations
children.description: The product uses a signal handler that shares state with other signal handlers, but it does not properly mask or prevent those signal handlers from being invoked while the original signal handler is still running.

children.name: Signal Handler Use of a Non-reentrant Function
children.description: The product defines a signal handler that calls a non-reentrant function.

children.name: Use of Singleton Pattern Without Synchronization in a Multithreaded Context
children.description: The product uses the singleton pattern when creating a resource within a multithreaded environment.

children.name: Use of getlogin() in Multithreaded Application
children.description: The product uses the getlogin() function in a multithreaded context, potentially causing it to return incorrect values.

children.name: Unsynchronized Access to Shared Data in a Multithreaded Context
children.description: The product does not properly synchronize shared data, such as static variables across threads, which can lead to undefined behavior and unpredictable data changes.

children.name: Call to Thread run() instead of start()
children.description: The product calls a thread's run() method instead of calling start(), which causes the code to run in the thread of the caller instead of the callee.

children.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Synchronization Primitives
children.description: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using thread synchronization primitives.

children.name: Sensitive Data Storage in Improperly Locked Memory
children.description: The product stores sensitive data in memory that is not locked, or that has been incorrectly locked, which might cause the memory to be written to swap files on disk by the virtual memory manager. This can make the data more accessible to external actors.

children.name: Double-Checked Locking
children.description: The product uses double-checked locking to access a resource without the overhead of explicit synchronization, but the locking is insufficient.

children.name: Use of a Non-reentrant Function in a Concurrent Context
children.description: The product calls a non-reentrant function in a concurrent context in which a competing code sequence (e.g. thread or signal handler) may have an opportunity to call the same function or otherwise influence its state.

children.name: Improper Locking
children.description: The product does not properly acquire or release a lock on a resource, leading to unexpected resource state changes and behaviors.

children.name: Permission Race Condition During Resource Copy
children.description: The product, while copying or cloning a resource, does not set the resource's permissions or access control until the copy is complete, leaving the resource exposed to other spheres while the copy is taking place.

children.name: Multiple Locks of a Critical Resource
children.description: The product locks a critical resource more times than intended, leading to an unexpected state in the system.

children.name: Multiple Unlocks of a Critical Resource
children.description: The product unlocks a critical resource more times than intended, leading to an unexpected state in the system.

children.name: Missing Synchronization
children.description: The product utilizes a shared resource in a concurrent manner but does not attempt to synchronize access to the resource.

children.name: Incorrect Synchronization
children.description: The product utilizes a shared resource in a concurrent manner, but it does not correctly synchronize access to the resource.

children.name: Signal Handler with Functionality that is not Asynchronous-Safe
children.description: The product defines a signal handler that contains code sequences that are not asynchronous-safe, i.e., the functionality is not reentrant, or it can be interrupted.

children.name: Signal Handler Function Associated with Multiple Signals
children.description: The product defines a function that is used as a handler for more than one signal.

children.name: Unlock of a Resource that is not Locked
children.description: The product attempts to unlock a resource that is not locked.

children.name: Deadlock
children.description: The product contains multiple threads or executable segments that are waiting for each other to release a necessary lock, resulting in deadlock.

children.name: Invokable Control Element in Multi-Thread Context with non-Final Static Storable or Member Element
children.description:
The code contains a function or method that
		 operates in a multi-threaded environment but owns an unsafe non-final
		                     static storable or member data element.

children.name: Synchronous Access of Remote Resource without Timeout
children.description: The code has a synchronous call to a remote resource, but there is no timeout for the call, or the timeout is set to infinite.

children.name: Singleton Class Instance Creation without Proper Locking or Synchronization
children.description: The product implements a Singleton design pattern but does not use appropriate locking or other synchronization mechanism to ensure that the singleton class is only instantiated once.

children.name: Race Condition for Write-Once Attributes
children.description: A write-once register in hardware design is programmable by an untrusted software component earlier than the trusted software component, resulting in a race condition issue.

children.name: Improper Lock Behavior After Power State Transition
children.description: Register lock bit protection disables changes to system configuration once the bit is set. Some of the protected registers or lock bits become programmable after power state transitions (e.g., Entry and wake from low power sleep modes) causing the system configuration to be changeable.

children.name: Hardware Internal or Debug Modes Allow Override of Locks
children.description: System configuration protection may be bypassed during debug mode.

children.name: Hardware Logic with Insecure De-Synchronization between Control and Data Channels
children.description: The hardware logic for error handling and security checks can incorrectly forward data before the security check is complete.

children.name: Hardware Logic Contains Race Conditions
children.description: A race condition in the hardware logic results in undermining security guarantees of the system.

children.name: Encryption
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to encryption.

children.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Data Transmission Without Encryption
children.description: Information sent over a network can be compromised while in transit. An attacker may be able to read or modify the contents if the data are sent in plaintext or are weakly encrypted.

children.name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
children.description: The product does not encrypt sensitive or critical information before storage or transmission.

children.name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext within a resource that might be accessible to another control sphere.

children.name: Cleartext Storage in a File or on Disk
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext in a file, or on disk.

children.name: Cleartext Storage in the Registry
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext in the registry.

children.name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in a Cookie
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext in a cookie.

children.name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in Memory
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext in memory.

children.name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in GUI
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext within the GUI.

children.name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in Executable
children.description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext in an executable.

children.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
children.description: The product transmits sensitive or security-critical data in cleartext in a communication channel that can be sniffed by unauthorized actors.

children.name: Use of a Key Past its Expiration Date
children.description: The product uses a cryptographic key or password past its expiration date, which diminishes its safety significantly by increasing the timing window for cracking attacks against that key.

children.name: Missing Cryptographic Step
children.description: The product does not implement a required step in a cryptographic algorithm, resulting in weaker encryption than advertised by the algorithm.

children.name: Inadequate Encryption Strength
children.description: The product stores or transmits sensitive data using an encryption scheme that is theoretically sound, but is not strong enough for the level of protection required.

children.name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
children.description: The product uses a broken or risky cryptographic algorithm or protocol.

children.name: Use of Weak Hash
children.description: The product uses an algorithm that produces a digest (output value) that does not meet security expectations for a hash function that allows an adversary to reasonably determine the original input (preimage attack), find another input that can produce the same hash (2nd preimage attack), or find mul

children.name: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
children.description: The product does not verify, or incorrectly verifies, the cryptographic signature for data.

children.name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
children.description: The Secure attribute for sensitive cookies in HTTPS sessions is not set.

children.name: Use of a One-Way Hash without a Salt
children.description: The product uses a one-way cryptographic hash against an input that should not be reversible, such as a password, but the product does not also use a salt as part of the input.

children.name: Use of a One-Way Hash with a Predictable Salt
children.description: The product uses a one-way cryptographic hash against an input that should not be reversible, such as a password, but the product uses a predictable salt as part of the input.

children.name: Use of RSA Algorithm without OAEP
children.description: The product uses the RSA algorithm but does not incorporate Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding (OAEP), which might weaken the encryption.

children.name: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort
children.description: The product generates a hash for a password, but it uses a scheme that does not provide a sufficient level of computational effort that would make password cracking attacks infeasible or expensive.

children.name: Use of a Cryptographic Primitive with a Risky Implementation
children.description: To fulfill the need for a cryptographic primitive, the product implements a cryptographic algorithm using a non-standard, unproven, or disallowed/non-compliant cryptographic implementation.

children.name: Reliance on HTTP instead of HTTPS
children.description: The product provides or relies on use of HTTP communications when HTTPS is available.

children.name: Exposed Resource
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to exposed resource.

children.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Entity Bean Declared Remote
children.description: When an application exposes a remote interface for an entity bean, it might also expose methods that get or set the bean's data. These methods could be leveraged to read sensitive information, or to change data in ways that violate the application's expectations, potentially leading to other vulnera

children.name: External Control of System or Configuration Setting
children.description: One or more system settings or configuration elements can be externally controlled by a user.

children.name: External Control of File Name or Path
children.description: The product allows user input to control or influence paths or file names that are used in filesystem operations.

children.name: Process Control
children.description: Executing commands or loading libraries from an untrusted source or in an untrusted environment can cause an application to execute malicious commands (and payloads) on behalf of an attacker.

children.name: Storage of File with Sensitive Data Under Web Root
children.description: The product stores sensitive data under the web document root with insufficient access control, which might make it accessible to untrusted parties.

children.name: Storage of File With Sensitive Data Under FTP Root
children.description: The product stores sensitive data under the FTP server root with insufficient access control, which might make it accessible to untrusted parties.

children.name: Passing Mutable Objects to an Untrusted Method
children.description: The product sends non-cloned mutable data as an argument to a method or function.

children.name: Returning a Mutable Object to an Untrusted Caller
children.description: Sending non-cloned mutable data as a return value may result in that data being altered or deleted by the calling function.

children.name: Insecure Temporary File
children.description: Creating and using insecure temporary files can leave application and system data vulnerable to attack.

children.name: Creation of Temporary File With Insecure Permissions
children.description: Opening temporary files without appropriate measures or controls can leave the file, its contents and any function that it impacts vulnerable to attack.

children.name: Creation of Temporary File in Directory with Insecure Permissions
children.description: The product creates a temporary file in a directory whose permissions allow unintended actors to determine the file's existence or otherwise access that file.

children.name: Transmission of Private Resources into a New Sphere ('Resource Leak')
children.description: The product makes resources available to untrusted parties when those resources are only intended to be accessed by the product.

children.name: Exposure of File Descriptor to Unintended Control Sphere ('File Descriptor Leak')
children.description: A process does not close sensitive file descriptors before invoking a child process, which allows the child to perform unauthorized I/O operations using those descriptors.

children.name: Untrusted Search Path
children.description: The product searches for critical resources using an externally-supplied search path that can point to resources that are not under the product's direct control.

children.name: Uncontrolled Search Path Element
children.description: The product uses a fixed or controlled search path to find resources, but one or more locations in that path can be under the control of unintended actors.

children.name: Unquoted Search Path or Element
children.description: The product uses a search path that contains an unquoted element, in which the element contains whitespace or other separators. This can cause the product to access resources in a parent path.

children.name: Unparsed Raw Web Content Delivery
children.description: The product stores raw content or supporting code under the web document root with an extension that is not specifically handled by the server.

children.name: External Control of Assumed-Immutable Web Parameter
children.description: The web application does not sufficiently verify inputs that are assumed to be immutable but are actually externally controllable, such as hidden form fields.

children.name: Exposure of Data Element to Wrong Session
children.description: The product does not sufficiently enforce boundaries between the states of different sessions, causing data to be provided to, or used by, the wrong session.

children.name: Public cloneable() Method Without Final ('Object Hijack')
children.description: A class has a cloneable() method that is not declared final, which allows an object to be created without calling the constructor. This can cause the object to be in an unexpected state.

children.name: Use of Inner Class Containing Sensitive Data
children.description: Inner classes are translated into classes that are accessible at package scope and may expose code that the programmer intended to keep private to attackers.

children.name: Critical Public Variable Without Final Modifier
children.description: The product has a critical public variable that is not final, which allows the variable to be modified to contain unexpected values.

children.name: Cloneable Class Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The code contains a class with sensitive data, but the class is cloneable. The data can then be accessed by cloning the class.

children.name: Serializable Class Containing Sensitive Data
children.description: The code contains a class with sensitive data, but the class does not explicitly deny serialization. The data can be accessed by serializing the class through another class.

children.name: Public Static Field Not Marked Final
children.description: An object contains a public static field that is not marked final, which might allow it to be modified in unexpected ways.

children.name: Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The code uses a cache that contains sensitive information, but the cache can be read by an actor outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Use of Web Browser Cache Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The web application does not use an appropriate caching policy that specifies the extent to which each web page and associated form fields should be cached.

children.name: Exposure of Version-Control Repository to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
children.description: The product stores a CVS, git, or other repository in a directory, archive, or other resource that is stored, transferred, or otherwise made accessible to unauthorized actors.

children.name: Exposure of Core Dump File to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
children.description: The product generates a core dump file in a directory, archive, or other resource that is stored, transferred, or otherwise made accessible to unauthorized actors.

children.name: Exposure of Access Control List Files to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
children.description: The product stores access control list files in a directory or other container that is accessible to actors outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Exposure of Backup File to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
children.description: A backup file is stored in a directory or archive that is made accessible to unauthorized actors.

children.name: Use of Persistent Cookies Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The web application uses persistent cookies, but the cookies contain sensitive information.

children.name: Files or Directories Accessible to External Parties
children.description: The product makes files or directories accessible to unauthorized actors, even though they should not be.

children.name: Command Shell in Externally Accessible Directory
children.description: A possible shell file exists in /cgi-bin/ or other accessible directories. This is extremely dangerous and can be used by an attacker to execute commands on the web server.

children.name: Reliance on Cookies without Validation and Integrity Checking
children.description: The product relies on the existence or values of cookies when performing security-critical operations, but it does not properly ensure that the setting is valid for the associated user.

children.name: Array Declared Public, Final, and Static
children.description: The product declares an array public, final, and static, which is not sufficient to prevent the array's contents from being modified.

children.name: finalize() Method Declared Public
children.description: The product violates secure coding principles for mobile code by declaring a finalize() method public.

children.name: Struts: Non-private Field in ActionForm Class
children.description: An ActionForm class contains a field that has not been declared private, which can be accessed without using a setter or getter.

children.name: Dangling Database Cursor ('Cursor Injection')
children.description: If a database cursor is not closed properly, then it could become accessible to other users while retaining the same privileges that were originally assigned, leaving the cursor "dangling."

children.name: External Control of Critical State Data
children.description: The product stores security-critical state information about its users, or the product itself, in a location that is accessible to unauthorized actors.

children.name: Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere
children.description: The product exposes a resource to the wrong control sphere, providing unintended actors with inappropriate access to the resource.

children.name: Access to Critical Private Variable via Public Method
children.description: The product defines a public method that reads or modifies a private variable.

children.name: Reliance on Cookies without Validation and Integrity Checking in a Security Decision
children.description: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of a cookie, but it does not properly ensure that the cookie is valid for the associated user.

children.name: Assumed-Immutable Data is Stored in Writable Memory
children.description: Immutable data, such as a first-stage bootloader, device identifiers, and "write-once" configuration settings are stored in writable memory that can be re-programmed or updated in the field.

children.name: Binding to an Unrestricted IP Address
children.description: The product assigns the address 0.0.0.0 for a database server, a cloud service/instance, or any computing resource that communicates remotely.

children.name: File Handling
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to file handling.

children.name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that is intended to identify a file or directory that is located underneath a restricted parent directory, but the product does not properly neutralize special elements within the pathname that can cause the pathname to resolve to a location th

children.name: Relative Path Traversal
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize sequences such as ".." that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '../filedir'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize "../" sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '/../filedir'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize "/../" sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '/dir/../filename'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize "/dir/../filename" sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: 'dir/../../filename'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize multiple internal "../" sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '..\filedir'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize "..\" sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '\..\filename'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize '\..\filename' (leading backslash dot dot) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '\dir\..\filename'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize '\dir\..\filename' (leading backslash dot dot) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: 'dir\..\..\filename'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize 'dir\..\..\filename' (multiple internal backslash dot dot) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '...' (Triple Dot)
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize '...' (triple dot) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '....' (Multiple Dot)
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize '....' (multiple dot) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '....//'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize '....//' (doubled dot dot slash) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '.../...//'
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize '.../...//' (doubled triple dot slash) sequences that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Absolute Path Traversal
children.description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that should be within a restricted directory, but it does not properly neutralize absolute path sequences such as "/abs/path" that can resolve to a location that is outside of that directory.

children.name: Path Traversal: '/absolute/pathname/here'
children.description: The product accepts input in the form of a slash absolute path ('/absolute/pathname/here') without appropriate validation, which can allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Traversal: '\absolute\pathname\here'
children.description: The product accepts input in the form of a backslash absolute path ('\absolute\pathname\here') without appropriate validation, which can allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Traversal: 'C:dirname'
children.description: The product accepts input that contains a drive letter or Windows volume letter ('C:dirname') that potentially redirects access to an unintended location or arbitrary file.

children.name: Path Traversal: '\\UNC\share\name\' (Windows UNC Share)
children.description: The product accepts input that identifies a Windows UNC share ('\\UNC\share\name') that potentially redirects access to an unintended location or arbitrary file.

children.name: Improper Resolution of Path Equivalence
children.description: The product is vulnerable to file system contents disclosure through path equivalence. Path equivalence involves the use of special characters in file and directory names. The associated manipulations are intended to generate multiple names for the same object.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'filename.' (Trailing Dot)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of trailing dot ('filedir.') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'filename....' (Multiple Trailing Dot)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple trailing dot ('filedir....') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'file.name' (Internal Dot)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of internal dot ('file.ordir') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'file...name' (Multiple Internal Dot)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple internal dot ('file...dir') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'filename ' (Trailing Space)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of trailing space ('filedir ') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: ' filename' (Leading Space)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of leading space (' filedir') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'file name' (Internal Whitespace)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of internal space ('file(SPACE)name') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'filename/' (Trailing Slash)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of trailing slash ('filedir/') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: '//multiple/leading/slash'
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple leading slash ('//multiple/leading/slash') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: '/multiple//internal/slash'
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple internal slash ('/multiple//internal/slash/') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: '/multiple/trailing/slash//'
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple trailing slash ('/multiple/trailing/slash//') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: '\multiple\\internal\backslash'
