children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple internal backslash ('\multiple\trailing\\slash') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'filedir\' (Trailing Backslash)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of trailing backslash ('filedir\') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: '/./' (Single Dot Directory)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of single dot directory exploit ('/./') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'filedir*' (Wildcard)
children.description: The product accepts path input in the form of asterisk wildcard ('filedir*') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.

children.name: Path Equivalence: 'fakedir/../realdir/filename'
children.description: The product contains protection mechanisms to restrict access to 'realdir/filename', but it constructs pathnames using external input in the form of 'fakedir/../realdir/filename' that are not handled by those mechanisms. This allows attackers to perform unauthorized actions against the targeted file

children.name: Path Equivalence: Windows 8.3 Filename
children.description: The product contains a protection mechanism that restricts access to a long filename on a Windows operating system, but it does not properly restrict access to the equivalent short "8.3" filename.

children.name: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access ('Link Following')
children.description: The product attempts to access a file based on the filename, but it does not properly prevent that filename from identifying a link or shortcut that resolves to an unintended resource.

children.name: UNIX Symbolic Link (Symlink) Following
children.description: The product, when opening a file or directory, does not sufficiently account for when the file is a symbolic link that resolves to a target outside of the intended control sphere. This could allow an attacker to cause the product to operate on unauthorized files.

children.name: UNIX Hard Link
children.description: The product, when opening a file or directory, does not sufficiently account for when the name is associated with a hard link to a target that is outside of the intended control sphere. This could allow an attacker to cause the product to operate on unauthorized files.

children.name: Windows Shortcut Following (.LNK)
children.description: The product, when opening a file or directory, does not sufficiently handle when the file is a Windows shortcut (.LNK) whose target is outside of the intended control sphere. This could allow an attacker to cause the product to operate on unauthorized files.

children.name: Windows Hard Link
children.description: The product, when opening a file or directory, does not sufficiently handle when the name is associated with a hard link to a target that is outside of the intended control sphere. This could allow an attacker to cause the product to operate on unauthorized files.

children.name: Improper Handling of File Names that Identify Virtual Resources
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles a file name that identifies a "virtual" resource that is not directly specified within the directory that is associated with the file name, causing the product to perform file-based operations on a resource that is not a file.

children.name: Improper Handling of Windows Device Names
children.description: The product constructs pathnames from user input, but it does not handle or incorrectly handles a pathname containing a Windows device name such as AUX or CON. This typically leads to denial of service or an information exposure when the application attempts to process the pathname as a regular file

children.name: Improper Handling of Windows ::DATA Alternate Data Stream
children.description: The product does not properly prevent access to, or detect usage of, alternate data streams (ADS).

children.name: Improper Handling of Apple HFS+ Alternate Data Stream Path
children.description: The product does not properly handle special paths that may identify the data or resource fork of a file on the HFS+ file system.

children.name: Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to improper check or handling of exceptional conditions.

children.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
children.description: The default error page of a web application should not display sensitive information about the product.

children.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
children.description: An ASP .NET application must enable custom error pages in order to prevent attackers from mining information from the framework's built-in responses.

children.name: Unchecked Return Value
children.description: The product does not check the return value from a method or function, which can prevent it from detecting unexpected states and conditions.

children.name: Detection of Error Condition Without Action
children.description: The product detects a specific error, but takes no actions to handle the error.

children.name: Unchecked Error Condition
children.description: [PLANNED FOR DEPRECATION. SEE MAINTENANCE NOTES AND CONSIDER CWE-252, CWE-248, OR CWE-1069.] Ignoring exceptions and other error conditions may allow an attacker to induce unexpected behavior unnoticed.

children.name: Unexpected Status Code or Return Value
children.description: The product does not properly check when a function or operation returns a value that is legitimate for the function, but is not expected by the product.

children.name: Missing Standardized Error Handling Mechanism
children.description: The product does not use a standardized method for handling errors throughout the code, which might introduce inconsistent error handling and resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions
children.description: The product does not properly anticipate or handle exceptional conditions that rarely occur during normal operation of the product.

children.name: Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions
children.description: The product does not check or incorrectly checks for unusual or exceptional conditions that are not expected to occur frequently during day to day operation of the product.

children.name: Improper Handling of Exceptional Conditions
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles an exceptional condition.

children.name: Missing Custom Error Page
children.description: The product does not return custom error pages to the user, possibly exposing sensitive information.

children.name: Improper Protection Against Voltage and Clock Glitches
children.description: The device does not contain or contains incorrectly implemented circuitry or sensors to detect and mitigate voltage and clock glitches and protect sensitive information or software contained on the device.

children.name: Improper Handling of Single Event Upsets
children.description: The hardware logic does not effectively handle when single-event upsets (SEUs) occur.

children.name: Improper Handling of Faults that Lead to Instruction Skips
children.description: The device is missing or incorrectly implements circuitry or sensors that detect and mitigate the skipping of security-critical CPU instructions when they occur.

children.name: Improper Handling of Hardware Behavior in Exceptionally Cold Environments
children.description:
A hardware device, or the firmware running on it, is
                missing or has incorrect protection features to maintain
                goals of security primitives when the device is cooled below
                standard operating temperatures.

children.name: Improper Handling of Physical or Environmental Conditions
children.description: The product does not properly handle unexpected physical or environmental conditions that occur naturally or are artificially induced.

children.name: Improper Input Validation
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to improper input validation.

children.name: Improper Input Validation
children.description:
The product receives input or data, but it does
        not validate or incorrectly validates that the input has the
        properties that are required to process the data safely and
        correctly.

children.name: Struts: Form Field Without Validator
children.description: The product has a form field that is not validated by a corresponding validation form, which can introduce other weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

children.name: Struts: Plug-in Framework not in Use
children.description: When an application does not use an input validation framework such as the Struts Validator, there is a greater risk of introducing weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

children.name: Struts: Unvalidated Action Form
children.description: Every Action Form must have a corresponding validation form.

children.name: Struts: Validator Turned Off
children.description: Automatic filtering via a Struts bean has been turned off, which disables the Struts Validator and custom validation logic. This exposes the application to other weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

children.name: Missing XML Validation
children.description: The product accepts XML from an untrusted source but does not validate the XML against the proper schema.

children.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Not Using Input Validation Framework
children.description: The ASP.NET application does not use an input validation framework.

children.name: Unchecked Input for Loop Condition
children.description: The product does not properly check inputs that are used for loop conditions, potentially leading to a denial of service or other consequences because of excessive looping.

children.name: Improper Validation of Function Hook Arguments
children.description: The product adds hooks to user-accessible API functions, but it does not properly validate the arguments. This could lead to resultant vulnerabilities.

children.name: Improper Address Validation in IOCTL with METHOD_NEITHER I/O Control Code
children.description: The product defines an IOCTL that uses METHOD_NEITHER for I/O, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates the addresses that are provided.

children.name: Improper Use of Validation Framework
children.description: The product does not use, or incorrectly uses, an input validation framework that is provided by the source language or an independent library.

children.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Improper Model Validation
children.description: The ASP.NET application does not use, or incorrectly uses, the model validation framework.

children.name: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input
children.description: The product receives input that is expected to specify a quantity (such as size or length), but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the quantity has the required properties.

children.name: Improper Validation of Specified Index, Position, or Offset in Input
children.description: The product receives input that is expected to specify an index, position, or offset into an indexable resource such as a buffer or file, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the specified index/position/offset has the required properties.

children.name: Improper Validation of Syntactic Correctness of Input
children.description: The product receives input that is expected to be well-formed - i.e., to comply with a certain syntax - but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input complies with the syntax.

children.name: Improper Validation of Specified Type of Input
children.description: The product receives input that is expected to be of a certain type, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input is actually of the expected type.

children.name: Improper Validation of Consistency within Input
children.description: The product receives a complex input with multiple elements or fields that must be consistent with each other, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input is actually consistent.

children.name: Improper Validation of Unsafe Equivalence in Input
children.description: The product receives an input value that is used as a resource identifier or other type of reference, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input is equivalent to a potentially-unsafe value.

children.name: Improper Neutralization
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to improper neutralization.

children.name: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output
children.description: The product prepares a structured message for communication with another component, but encoding or escaping of the data is either missing or done incorrectly. As a result, the intended structure of the message is not preserved.

children.name: Improper Output Neutralization for Logs
children.description: The product constructs a log message from external input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements when the message is written to a log file.

children.name: Improper Handling of Length Parameter Inconsistency
children.description: The product parses a formatted message or structure, but it does not handle or incorrectly handles a length field that is inconsistent with the actual length of the associated data.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as control elements or syntactic markers when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Delimiters
children.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes delimiters.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Parameter/Argument Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as parameter or argument delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Value Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as value delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Record Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as record delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Line Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as line delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Section Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as section delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Expression/Command Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as expression or command delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Input Terminators
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as input terminators when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Input Leaders
children.description: The product does not properly handle when a leading character or sequence ("leader") is missing or malformed, or if multiple leaders are used when only one should be allowed.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Quoting Syntax
children.description: Quotes injected into a product can be used to compromise a system. As data are parsed, an injected/absent/duplicate/malformed use of quotes may cause the process to take unexpected actions.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Escape, Meta, or Control Sequences
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as escape, meta, or control character sequences when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Comment Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as comment delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Macro Symbols
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as macro symbols when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Substitution Characters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as substitution characters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Variable Name Delimiters
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as variable name delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Wildcards or Matching Symbols
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as wildcards or matching symbols when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Whitespace
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as whitespace when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Failure to Sanitize Paired Delimiters
children.description: The product does not properly handle the characters that are used to mark the beginning and ending of a group of entities, such as parentheses, brackets, and braces.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Null Byte or NUL Character
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes NUL characters or null bytes when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Handling of Invalid Use of Special Elements
children.description: The product does not properly filter, remove, quote, or otherwise manage the invalid use of special elements in user-controlled input, which could cause adverse effect on its behavior and integrity.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Leading Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes leading special elements that could be interpreted in unexpected ways when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Multiple Leading Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes multiple leading special elements that could be interpreted in unexpected ways when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Trailing Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes trailing special elements that could be interpreted in unexpected ways when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Multiple Trailing Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes multiple trailing special elements that could be interpreted in unexpected ways when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Internal Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes internal special elements that could be interpreted in unexpected ways when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Multiple Internal Special Elements
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes multiple internal special elements that could be interpreted in unexpected ways when they are sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Handling of Missing Special Element
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not handle or incorrectly handles when an expected special element is missing.

children.name: Improper Handling of Additional Special Element
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not handle or incorrectly handles when an additional unexpected special element is provided.

children.name: Improper Handling of Inconsistent Special Elements
children.description: The product does not properly handle input in which an inconsistency exists between two or more special characters or reserved words.

children.name: Improper Null Termination
children.description: The product does not terminate or incorrectly terminates a string or array with a null character or equivalent terminator.

children.name: Encoding Error
children.description: The product does not properly encode or decode the data, resulting in unexpected values.

children.name: Improper Handling of Alternate Encoding
children.description: The product does not properly handle when an input uses an alternate encoding that is valid for the control sphere to which the input is being sent.

children.name: Double Decoding of the Same Data
children.description: The product decodes the same input twice, which can limit the effectiveness of any protection mechanism that occurs in between the decoding operations.

children.name: Improper Handling of Mixed Encoding
children.description: The product does not properly handle when the same input uses several different (mixed) encodings.

children.name: Improper Handling of Unicode Encoding
children.description: The product does not properly handle when an input contains Unicode encoding.

children.name: Improper Handling of URL Encoding (Hex Encoding)
children.description: The product does not properly handle when all or part of an input has been URL encoded.

children.name: Improper Handling of Syntactically Invalid Structure
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles input that is not syntactically well-formed with respect to the associated specification.

children.name: Improper Handling of Values
children.description: The product does not properly handle when the expected number of values for parameters, fields, or arguments is not provided in input, or if those values are undefined.

children.name: Improper Handling of Missing Values
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when a parameter, field, or argument name is specified, but the associated value is missing, i.e. it is empty, blank, or null.

children.name: Improper Handling of Extra Values
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when more values are provided than expected.

children.name: Improper Handling of Undefined Values
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when a value is not defined or supported for the associated parameter, field, or argument name.

children.name: Improper Handling of Parameters
children.description: The product does not properly handle when the expected number of parameters, fields, or arguments is not provided in input, or if those parameters are undefined.

children.name: Failure to Handle Missing Parameter
children.description: If too few arguments are sent to a function, the function will still pop the expected number of arguments from the stack. Potentially, a variable number of arguments could be exhausted in a function as well.

children.name: Improper Handling of Extra Parameters
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when the number of parameters, fields, or arguments with the same name exceeds the expected amount.

children.name: Improper Handling of Undefined Parameters
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when a particular parameter, field, or argument name is not defined or supported by the product.

children.name: Improper Handling of Structural Elements
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles inputs that are related to complex structures.

children.name: Improper Handling of Incomplete Structural Elements
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when a particular structural element is not completely specified.

children.name: Failure to Handle Incomplete Element
children.description: The product does not properly handle when a particular element is not completely specified.

children.name: Improper Handling of Inconsistent Structural Elements
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when two or more structural elements should be consistent, but are not.

children.name: Improper Handling of Unexpected Data Type
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles when a particular element is not the expected type, e.g. it expects a digit (0-9) but is provided with a letter (A-Z).

children.name: Deletion of Data Structure Sentinel
children.description: The accidental deletion of a data-structure sentinel can cause serious programming logic problems.

children.name: Addition of Data Structure Sentinel
children.description: The accidental addition of a data-structure sentinel can cause serious programming logic problems.

children.name: Null Byte Interaction Error (Poison Null Byte)
children.description: The product does not properly handle null bytes or NUL characters when passing data between different representations or components.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of HTTP Headers for Scripting Syntax
children.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes web scripting syntax in HTTP headers that can be used by web browser components that can process raw headers, such as Flash.

children.name: Improper Neutralization
children.description: The product does not ensure or incorrectly ensures that structured messages or data are well-formed and that certain security properties are met before being read from an upstream component or sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Filtering of Special Elements
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but does not filter or incorrectly filters special elements before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Incomplete Filtering of Special Elements
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but does not completely filter special elements before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Incomplete Filtering of One or More Instances of Special Elements
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but does not completely filter one or more instances of special elements before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Only Filtering One Instance of a Special Element
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but only filters a single instance of a special element before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Incomplete Filtering of Multiple Instances of Special Elements
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but does not filter all instances of a special element before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Only Filtering Special Elements at a Specified Location
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but only accounts for special elements at a specified location, thereby missing remaining special elements that may exist before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Only Filtering Special Elements Relative to a Marker
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but only accounts for special elements positioned relative to a marker (e.g. "at the beginning/end of a string; the second argument"), thereby missing remaining special elements that may exist before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Only Filtering Special Elements at an Absolute Position
children.description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but only accounts for special elements at an absolute position (e.g. "byte number 10"), thereby missing remaining special elements that may exist before sending it to a downstream component.

children.name: Inappropriate Encoding for Output Context
children.description: The product uses or specifies an encoding when generating output to a downstream component, but the specified encoding is not the same as the encoding that is expected by the downstream component.

children.name: Incorrect Calculation
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to incorrect calculation.

children.name: Wrap-around Error
children.description: Wrap around errors occur whenever a value is incremented past the maximum value for its type and therefore "wraps around" to a very small, negative, or undefined value.

children.name: Incorrect Calculation of Multi-Byte String Length
children.description: The product does not correctly calculate the length of strings that can contain wide or multi-byte characters.

children.name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
children.description:
The product performs a calculation that can
         produce an integer overflow or wraparound when the logic
         assumes that the resulting value will always be larger than
         the original value. This occurs when an integer value is
         incremented to a value that is too large to st

children.name: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)
children.description: The product subtracts one value from another, such that the result is less than the minimum allowable integer value, which produces a value that is not equal to the correct result.

children.name: Off-by-one Error
children.description: A product calculates or uses an incorrect maximum or minimum value that is 1 more, or 1 less, than the correct value.

children.name: Divide By Zero
children.description: The product divides a value by zero.

children.name: Use of sizeof() on a Pointer Type
children.description: The code calls sizeof() on a pointer type, which can be an incorrect calculation if the programmer intended to determine the size of the data that is being pointed to.

children.name: Incorrect Pointer Scaling
children.description: In C and C++, one may often accidentally refer to the wrong memory due to the semantics of when math operations are implicitly scaled.

children.name: Use of Pointer Subtraction to Determine Size
children.description: The product subtracts one pointer from another in order to determine size, but this calculation can be incorrect if the pointers do not exist in the same memory chunk.

children.name: Incorrect Calculation
children.description: The product performs a calculation that generates incorrect or unintended results that are later used in security-critical decisions or resource management.

children.name: Incorrect Bitwise Shift of Integer
children.description: An integer value is specified to be shifted by a negative amount or an amount greater than or equal to the number of bits contained in the value causing an unexpected or indeterminate result.

children.name: Insufficient Precision or Accuracy of a Real Number
children.description: The product processes a real number with an implementation in which the number's representation does not preserve required accuracy and precision in its fractional part, causing an incorrect result.

children.name: Injection
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to injection.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Output Used by a Downstream Component ('Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of a command, data structure, or record using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify how it is parsed or interpreted when it is sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Failure to Sanitize Special Elements into a Different Plane (Special Element Injection)
children.description: The product does not adequately filter user-controlled input for special elements with control implications.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Equivalent Special Elements
children.description: The product correctly neutralizes certain special elements, but it improperly neutralizes equivalent special elements.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command ('Command Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of a command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended command when it is sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of an OS command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended OS command when it is sent to a downstream component.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
children.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input before it is placed in output that is used as a web page that is served to other users.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Script-Related HTML Tags in a Web Page (Basic XSS)
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special characters such as "<", ">", and "&" that could be interpreted as web-scripting elements when they are sent to a downstream component that processes web pages.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Script in an Error Message Web Page
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special characters that could be interpreted as web-scripting elements when they are sent to an error page.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Script in Attributes of IMG Tags in a Web Page
children.description: The web application does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes scripting elements within attributes of HTML IMG tags, such as the src attribute.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Script in Attributes in a Web Page
children.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes "javascript:" or other URIs from dangerous attributes within tags, such as onmouseover, onload, onerror, or style.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Encoded URI Schemes in a Web Page
children.description: The web application improperly neutralizes user-controlled input for executable script disguised with URI encodings.

children.name: Doubled Character XSS Manipulations
children.description: The web application does not filter user-controlled input for executable script disguised using doubling of the involved characters.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Invalid Characters in Identifiers in Web Pages
children.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes invalid characters or byte sequences in the middle of tag names, URI schemes, and other identifiers.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Alternate XSS Syntax
children.description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controlled input for alternate script syntax.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Argument Delimiters in a Command ('Argument Injection')
children.description:
The product constructs a string for a command to be executed by a separate component
in another control sphere, but it does not properly delimit the
intended arguments, options, or switches within that command string.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended SQL command when it is sent to a downstream component. Without sufficient removal o

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query ('LDAP Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of an LDAP query using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended LDAP query when it is sent to a downstream component.

children.name: XML Injection (aka Blind XPath Injection)
children.description: The product does not properly neutralize special elements that are used in XML, allowing attackers to modify the syntax, content, or commands of the XML before it is processed by an end system.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences ('CRLF Injection')
children.description: The product uses CRLF (carriage return line feeds) as a special element, e.g. to separate lines or records, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes CRLF sequences from inputs.

children.name: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of a code segment using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the syntax or behavior of the intended code segment.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes code syntax before using the input in a dynamic evaluation call (e.g. "eval").

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Statically Saved Code ('Static Code Injection')
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes code syntax before inserting the input into an executable resource, such as a library, configuration file, or template.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Server-Side Includes (SSI) Within a Web Page
children.description: The product generates a web page, but does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input that could be interpreted as a server-side include (SSI) directive.

children.name: Improper Control of Resource Identifiers ('Resource Injection')
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not restrict or incorrectly restricts the input before it is used as an identifier for a resource that may be outside the intended sphere of control.

children.name: Struts: Duplicate Validation Forms
children.description: The product uses multiple validation forms with the same name, which might cause the Struts Validator to validate a form that the programmer does not expect.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Request/Response Splitting')
children.description: The product receives data from an HTTP agent/component (e.g., web server, proxy, browser, etc.), but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes CR and LF characters before the data is included in outgoing HTTP headers.

children.name: SQL Injection: Hibernate
children.description: Using Hibernate to execute a dynamic SQL statement built with user-controlled input can allow an attacker to modify the statement's meaning or to execute arbitrary SQL commands.

children.name: Variable Extraction Error
children.description: The product uses external input to determine the names of variables into which information is extracted, without verifying that the names of the specified variables are valid. This could cause the program to overwrite unintended variables.

children.name: Executable Regular Expression Error
children.description: The product uses a regular expression that either (1) contains an executable component with user-controlled inputs, or (2) allows a user to enable execution by inserting pattern modifiers.

children.name: Dynamic Variable Evaluation
children.description: In a language where the user can influence the name of a variable at runtime, if the variable names are not controlled, an attacker can read or write to arbitrary variables, or access arbitrary functions.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Names for Files and Other Resources
children.description: The product constructs the name of a file or other resource using input from an upstream component, but it does not restrict or incorrectly restricts the resulting name.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Data within XPath Expressions ('XPath Injection')
children.description: The product uses external input to dynamically construct an XPath expression used to retrieve data from an XML database, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes that input. This allows an attacker to control the structure of the query.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Data within XQuery Expressions ('XQuery Injection')
children.description: The product uses external input to dynamically construct an XQuery expression used to retrieve data from an XML database, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes that input. This allows an attacker to control the structure of the query.

children.name: Incomplete Denylist to Cross-Site Scripting
children.description: The product uses a denylist-based protection mechanism to defend against XSS attacks, but the denylist is incomplete, allowing XSS variants to succeed.

children.name: Use of Multiple Resources with Duplicate Identifier
children.description: The product uses multiple resources that can have the same identifier, in a context in which unique identifiers are required.

children.name: Improper Control of Dynamically-Identified Variables
children.description: The product does not properly restrict reading from or writing to dynamically-identified variables.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an Expression Language Statement ('Expression Language Injection')
children.description: The product constructs all or part of an expression language (EL) statement in a framework such as a Java Server Page (JSP) using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended EL statement

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Data Query Logic
children.description: The product generates a query intended to access or manipulate data in a data store such as a database, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that can modify the intended logic of the query.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Formula Elements in a CSV File
children.description: The product saves user-provided information into a Comma-Separated Value (CSV) file, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as a command when the file is opened by a spreadsheet product.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in a Template Engine
children.description: The product uses a template engine to insert or process externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements or syntax that can be interpreted as template expressions or other code directives when processed by the engine.

children.name: Improper Validation of Generative AI Output
children.description:
The product invokes a generative AI/ML
			component whose behaviors and outputs cannot be directly
			controlled, but the product does not validate or
			insufficiently validates the outputs to ensure that they
			align with the intended security, content, or privacy
			policy.

children.name: Improper Neutralization of Input Used for LLM Prompting
children.description:
The product uses externally-provided data to build prompts provided to
large language models (LLMs), but the way these prompts are constructed
causes the LLM to fail to distinguish between user-supplied inputs and
developer provided system directives.

children.name: Insufficient Control Flow Management
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to insufficient control flow management.

children.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Early Validation
children.description: The product validates input before applying protection mechanisms that modify the input, which could allow an attacker to bypass the validation via dangerous inputs that only arise after the modification.

children.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Validate Before Canonicalize
children.description: The product validates input before it is canonicalized, which prevents the product from detecting data that becomes invalid after the canonicalization step.

children.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Validate Before Filter
children.description: The product validates data before it has been filtered, which prevents the product from detecting data that becomes invalid after the filtering step.

children.name: Uncaught Exception
children.description: An exception is thrown from a function, but it is not caught.

children.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Use of System.exit()
children.description: A J2EE application uses System.exit(), which also shuts down its container.

children.name: Use of NullPointerException Catch to Detect NULL Pointer Dereference
children.description: Catching NullPointerException should not be used as an alternative to programmatic checks to prevent dereferencing a null pointer.

children.name: Declaration of Catch for Generic Exception
children.description: Catching overly broad exceptions promotes complex error handling code that is more likely to contain security vulnerabilities.

children.name: Declaration of Throws for Generic Exception
children.description: The product throws or raises an overly broad exceptions that can hide important details and produce inappropriate responses to certain conditions.

children.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Early Amplification
children.description: The product allows an entity to perform a legitimate but expensive operation before authentication or authorization has taken place.

children.name: Deployment of Wrong Handler
children.description: The wrong "handler" is assigned to process an object.

children.name: Missing Handler
children.description: A handler is not available or implemented.

children.name: Non-exit on Failed Initialization
children.description: The product does not exit or otherwise modify its operation when security-relevant errors occur during initialization, such as when a configuration file has a format error or a hardware security module (HSM) cannot be activated, which can cause the product to execute in a less secure fashion than in

children.name: Use of Incorrect Operator
children.description: The product accidentally uses the wrong operator, which changes the logic in security-relevant ways.

children.name: Assigning instead of Comparing
children.description: The code uses an operator for assignment when the intention was to perform a comparison.

children.name: Comparing instead of Assigning
children.description: The code uses an operator for comparison when the intention was to perform an assignment.

children.name: Incorrect Block Delimitation
children.description: The code does not explicitly delimit a block that is intended to contain 2 or more statements, creating a logic error.

children.name: Return Inside Finally Block
children.description: The code has a return statement inside a finally block, which will cause any thrown exception in the try block to be discarded.

children.name: Uncaught Exception in Servlet
children.description: The Servlet does not catch all exceptions, which may reveal sensitive debugging information.

children.name: Reachable Assertion
children.description: The product contains an assert() or similar statement that can be triggered by an attacker, which leads to an application exit or other behavior that is more severe than necessary.

children.name: Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation
children.description: The code contains a control flow path that does not reflect the algorithm that the path is intended to implement, leading to incorrect behavior any time this path is navigated.

children.name: Uncontrolled Recursion
children.description: The product does not properly control the amount of recursion that takes place,  consuming excessive resources, such as allocated memory or the program stack.

children.name: Insufficient Control Flow Management
children.description: The code does not sufficiently manage its control flow during execution, creating conditions in which the control flow can be modified in unexpected ways.

children.name: Incorrect Behavior Order
children.description: The product performs multiple related behaviors, but the behaviors are performed in the wrong order in ways that may produce resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Execution After Redirect (EAR)
children.description: The web application sends a redirect to another location, but instead of exiting, it executes additional code.

children.name: Incorrect Control Flow Scoping
children.description: The product does not properly return control flow to the proper location after it has completed a task or detected an unusual condition.

children.name: Incorrect Short Circuit Evaluation
children.description: The product contains a conditional statement with multiple logical expressions in which one of the non-leading expressions may produce side effects. This may lead to an unexpected state in the program after the execution of the conditional, because short-circuiting logic may prevent the side effects

children.name: Operator Precedence Logic Error
children.description: The product uses an expression in which operator precedence causes incorrect logic to be used.

children.name: Improper Control of Interaction Frequency
children.description: The product does not properly limit the number or frequency of interactions that it has with an actor, such as the number of incoming requests.

children.name: Excessive Iteration
children.description: The product performs an iteration or loop without sufficiently limiting the number of times that the loop is executed.

children.name: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop')
children.description: The product contains an iteration or loop with an exit condition that cannot be reached, i.e., an infinite loop.

children.name: Improper Enforcement of a Single, Unique Action
children.description: The product requires that an actor should only be able to perform an action once, or to have only one unique action, but the product does not enforce or improperly enforces this restriction.

children.name: Improper Enforcement of Behavioral Workflow
children.description: The product supports a session in which more than one behavior must be performed by an actor, but it does not properly ensure that the actor performs the behaviors in the required sequence.

children.name: DMA Device Enabled Too Early in Boot Phase
children.description: The product enables a Direct Memory Access (DMA) capable device before the security configuration settings are established, which allows an attacker to extract data from or gain privileges on the product.

children.name: Power-On of Untrusted Execution Core Before Enabling Fabric Access Control
children.description: The product enables components that contain untrusted firmware before memory and fabric access controls have been enabled.

children.name: Unintended Reentrant Invocation of Non-reentrant Code Via Nested Calls
children.description: The product invokes code that is believed to be reentrant, but the code performs a call that unintentionally produces a nested invocation of the non-reentrant code.

children.name: Access Control Check Implemented After Asset is Accessed
children.description: A product's hardware-based access control check occurs after the asset has been accessed.

children.name: Sequence of Processor Instructions Leads to Unexpected Behavior
children.description: Specific combinations of processor instructions lead to undesirable behavior such as locking the processor until a hard reset performed.

children.name: Use of Blocking Code in Single-threaded, Non-blocking Context
children.description:
The product uses a non-blocking model that relies on a single threaded process
			for features such as scalability, but it contains code that can block when it is invoked.

children.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to insufficient verification of data authenticity.

children.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
children.description: The product does not sufficiently verify the origin or authenticity of data, in a way that causes it to accept invalid data.

children.name: Origin Validation Error
children.description: The product does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.

children.name: Use of Less Trusted Source
children.description: The product has two different sources of the same data or information, but it uses the source that has less support for verification, is less trusted, or is less resistant to attack.

children.name: Acceptance of Extraneous Untrusted Data With Trusted Data
children.description: The product, when processing trusted data, accepts any untrusted data that is also included with the trusted data, treating the untrusted data as if it were trusted.

children.name: Insufficient Type Distinction
children.description: The product does not properly distinguish between different types of elements in a way that leads to insecure behavior.

children.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
children.description: The web application does not, or cannot, sufficiently verify whether a request was intentionally provided by the user who sent the request, which could have originated from an unauthorized actor.

children.name: Missing Support for Integrity Check
children.description: The product uses a transmission protocol that does not include a mechanism for verifying the integrity of the data during transmission, such as a checksum.

children.name: Improper Validation of Integrity Check Value
children.description: The product does not validate or incorrectly validates the integrity check values or "checksums" of a message. This may prevent it from detecting if the data has been modified or corrupted in transmission.

children.name: Trust of System Event Data
children.description: Security based on event locations are insecure and can be spoofed.

children.name: Download of Code Without Integrity Check
children.description: The product downloads source code or an executable from a remote location and executes the code without sufficiently verifying the origin and integrity of the code.

children.name: Incomplete Identification of Uploaded File Variables (PHP)
children.description: The PHP application uses an old method for processing uploaded files by referencing the four global variables that are set for each file (e.g. $varname, $varname_size, $varname_name, $varname_type). These variables could be overwritten by attackers, causing the application to process unauthorized fi

children.name: Reliance on File Name or Extension of Externally-Supplied File
children.description: The product allows a file to be uploaded, but it relies on the file name or extension of the file to determine the appropriate behaviors. This could be used by attackers to cause the file to be misclassified and processed in a dangerous fashion.

children.name: Reliance on Obfuscation or Encryption of Security-Relevant Inputs without Integrity Checking
children.description: The product uses obfuscation or encryption of inputs that should not be mutable by an external actor, but the product does not use integrity checks to detect if those inputs have been modified.

children.name: Improper Enforcement of Message Integrity During Transmission in a Communication Channel
children.description: The product establishes a communication channel with an endpoint and receives a message from that endpoint, but it does not sufficiently ensure that the message was not modified during transmission.

children.name: Missing Source Correlation of Multiple Independent Data
children.description: The product relies on one source of data, preventing the ability to detect if an adversary has compromised a data source.

children.name: Missing Origin Validation in WebSockets
children.description: The product uses a WebSocket, but it does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.

children.name: Memory Safety
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to memory safety.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
children.description: The product performs operations on a memory buffer, but it reads from or writes to a memory location outside the buffer's intended boundary. This may result in read or write operations on unexpected memory locations that could be linked to other variables, data structures, or internal program data.

children.name: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
children.description: The product copies an input buffer to an output buffer without verifying that the size of the input buffer is less than the size of the output buffer.

children.name: Stack-based Buffer Overflow
children.description: A stack-based buffer overflow condition is a condition where the buffer being overwritten is allocated on the stack (i.e., is a local variable or, rarely, a parameter to a function).

children.name: Heap-based Buffer Overflow
children.description: A heap overflow condition is a buffer overflow, where the buffer that can be overwritten is allocated in the heap portion of memory, generally meaning that the buffer was allocated using a routine such as malloc().

children.name: Write-what-where Condition
children.description: Any condition where the attacker has the ability to write an arbitrary value to an arbitrary location, often as the result of a buffer overflow.

children.name: Buffer Underwrite ('Buffer Underflow')
children.description: The product writes to a buffer using an index or pointer that references a memory location prior to the beginning of the buffer.

children.name: Out-of-bounds Read
children.description: The product reads data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer.

children.name: Buffer Over-read
children.description: The product reads from a buffer using buffer access mechanisms such as indexes or pointers that reference memory locations after the targeted buffer.

children.name: Buffer Under-read
children.description: The product reads from a buffer using buffer access mechanisms such as indexes or pointers that reference memory locations prior to the targeted buffer.

children.name: Improper Validation of Array Index
children.description: The product uses untrusted input when calculating or using an array index, but the product does not validate or incorrectly validates the index to ensure the index references a valid position within the array.

children.name: Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size
children.description: The product does not correctly calculate the size to be used when allocating a buffer, which could lead to a buffer overflow.

children.name: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
children.description: The product uses a function that accepts a format string as an argument, but the format string originates from an external source.

children.name: Reliance on Data/Memory Layout
children.description: The product makes invalid assumptions about how protocol data or memory is organized at a lower level, resulting in unintended program behavior.

children.name: Use of Incorrect Byte Ordering
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not account for byte ordering (e.g. big-endian and little-endian) when processing the input, causing an incorrect number or value to be used.

children.name: Improper Clearing of Heap Memory Before Release ('Heap Inspection')
children.description: Using realloc() to resize buffers that store sensitive information can leave the sensitive information exposed to attack, because it is not removed from memory.

children.name: Missing Release of Memory after Effective Lifetime
children.description: The product does not sufficiently track and release allocated memory after it has been used, making the memory unavailable for reallocation and reuse.

children.name: Double Free
children.description: The product calls free() twice on the same memory address.

children.name: Use After Free
children.description: The product reuses or references memory after it has been freed. At some point afterward, the memory may be allocated again and saved in another pointer, while the original pointer references a location somewhere within the new allocation. Any operations using the original pointer are no longer vali

children.name: Return of Pointer Value Outside of Expected Range
children.description: A function can return a pointer to memory that is outside of the buffer that the pointer is expected to reference.

children.name: Return of Stack Variable Address
children.description: A function returns the address of a stack variable, which will cause unintended program behavior, typically in the form of a crash.

children.name: Assignment of a Fixed Address to a Pointer
children.description: The product sets a pointer to a specific address other than NULL or 0.

children.name: Free of Memory not on the Heap
children.description: The product calls free() on a pointer to memory that was not allocated using associated heap allocation functions such as malloc(), calloc(), or realloc().

children.name: Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow
children.description: The product performs a calculation to determine how much memory to allocate, but an integer overflow can occur that causes less memory to be allocated than expected, leading to a buffer overflow.

children.name: Unchecked Return Value to NULL Pointer Dereference
children.description: The product does not check for an error after calling a function that can return with a NULL pointer if the function fails, which leads to a resultant NULL pointer dereference.

children.name: Free of Pointer not at Start of Buffer
children.description: The product calls free() on a pointer to a memory resource that was allocated on the heap, but the pointer is not at the start of the buffer.

children.name: Mismatched Memory Management Routines
children.description: The product attempts to return a memory resource to the system, but it calls a release function that is not compatible with the function that was originally used to allocate that resource.

children.name: Release of Invalid Pointer or Reference
children.description: The product attempts to return a memory resource to the system, but it calls the wrong release function or calls the appropriate release function incorrectly.

children.name: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer
children.description: The product reads or writes to a buffer using an index or pointer that references a memory location prior to the beginning of the buffer.

children.name: Out-of-bounds Write
children.description: The product writes data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer.

children.name: Access of Memory Location After End of Buffer
children.description: The product reads or writes to a buffer using an index or pointer that references a memory location after the end of the buffer.

children.name: Memory Allocation with Excessive Size Value
children.description: The product allocates memory based on an untrusted, large size value, but it does not ensure that the size is within expected limits, allowing arbitrary amounts of memory to be allocated.

children.name: Buffer Access with Incorrect Length Value
children.description: The product uses a sequential operation to read or write a buffer, but it uses an incorrect length value that causes it to access memory that is outside of the bounds of the buffer.

children.name: Buffer Access Using Size of Source Buffer
children.description: The product uses the size of a source buffer when reading from or writing to a destination buffer, which may cause it to access memory that is outside of the bounds of the buffer.

children.name: Untrusted Pointer Dereference
children.description: The product obtains a value from an untrusted source, converts this value to a pointer, and dereferences the resulting pointer.

children.name: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset
children.description: The product performs pointer arithmetic on a valid pointer, but it uses an offset that can point outside of the intended range of valid memory locations for the resulting pointer.

children.name: Access of Uninitialized Pointer
children.description: The product accesses or uses a pointer that has not been initialized.

children.name: Expired Pointer Dereference
children.description: The product dereferences a pointer that contains a location for memory that was previously valid, but is no longer valid.

children.name: Poor Coding Practices
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to poor coding practices.

children.name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Creating Debug Binary
children.description: Debugging messages help attackers learn about the system and plan a form of attack.

children.name: Struts: Incomplete validate() Method Definition
children.description: The product has a validator form that either does not define a validate() method, or defines a validate() method but does not call super.validate().

children.name: Struts: Form Bean Does Not Extend Validation Class
children.description: If a form bean does not extend an ActionForm subclass of the Validator framework, it can expose the application to other weaknesses related to insufficient input validation.

children.name: Struts: Unused Validation Form
children.description: An unused validation form indicates that validation logic is not up-to-date.

children.name: Struts: Validator Without Form Field
children.description: Validation fields that do not appear in forms they are associated with indicate that the validation logic is out of date.

children.name: Direct Use of Unsafe JNI
children.description: When a Java application uses the Java Native Interface (JNI) to call code written in another programming language, it can expose the application to weaknesses in that code, even if those weaknesses cannot occur in Java.

children.name: Use of Inherently Dangerous Function
children.description: The product calls a function that can never be guaranteed to work safely.

children.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Management of Connections
children.description: The J2EE application directly manages connections, instead of using the container's connection management facilities.

children.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Use of Sockets
children.description: The J2EE application directly uses sockets instead of using framework method calls.

children.name: Incorrect Check of Function Return Value
children.description: The product incorrectly checks a return value from a function, which prevents it from detecting errors or exceptional conditions.

children.name: Improperly Implemented Security Check for Standard
children.description: The product does not implement or incorrectly implements one or more security-relevant checks as specified by the design of a standardized algorithm, protocol, or technique.

children.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Use of Threads
children.description: Thread management in a Web application is forbidden in some circumstances and is always highly error prone.

children.name: Missing Report of Error Condition
children.description: The product encounters an error but does not provide a status code or return value to indicate that an error has occurred.

children.name: Return of Wrong Status Code
children.description: A function or operation returns an incorrect return value or status code that does not indicate the true result of execution, causing the product to modify its behavior based on the incorrect result.

children.name: Expected Behavior Violation
children.description: A feature, API, or function does not perform according to its specification.

children.name: UI Discrepancy for Security Feature
children.description: The user interface does not correctly enable or configure a security feature, but the interface provides feedback that causes the user to believe that the feature is in a secure state.

children.name: Obsolete Feature in UI
children.description: A UI function is obsolete and the product does not warn the user.

children.name: The UI Performs the Wrong Action
children.description: The UI performs the wrong action with respect to the user's request.

children.name: User Interface (UI) Misrepresentation of Critical Information
children.description: The user interface (UI) does not properly represent critical information to the user, allowing the information - or its source - to be obscured or spoofed. This is often a component in phishing attacks.

children.name: Duplicate Key in Associative List (Alist)
children.description: Duplicate keys in associative lists can lead to non-unique keys being mistaken for an error.

children.name: Use of Function with Inconsistent Implementations
children.description: The code uses a function that has inconsistent implementations across operating systems and versions.

children.name: Undefined Behavior for Input to API
children.description: The behavior of this function is undefined unless its control parameter is set to a specific value.

children.name: NULL Pointer Dereference
children.description: The product dereferences a pointer that it expects to be valid but is NULL.

children.name: Use of Obsolete Function
children.description: The code uses deprecated or obsolete functions, which suggests that the code has not been actively reviewed or maintained.

children.name: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
children.description: The product omits a break statement within a switch or similar construct, causing code associated with multiple conditions to execute. This can cause problems when the programmer only intended to execute code associated with one condition.

children.name: Active Debug Code
children.description: The product is released with debugging code still enabled or active.

children.name: Embedded Malicious Code
children.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.

children.name: Trojan Horse
children.description: The product appears to contain benign or useful functionality, but it also contains code that is hidden from normal operation that violates the intended security policy of the user or the system administrator.

children.name: Non-Replicating Malicious Code
children.description: Non-replicating malicious code only resides on the target system or product that is attacked; it does not attempt to spread to other systems.

children.name: Replicating Malicious Code (Virus or Worm)
children.description: Replicating malicious code, including viruses and worms, will attempt to attack other systems once it has successfully compromised the target system or the product.

children.name: Trapdoor
children.description: A trapdoor is a hidden piece of code that responds to a special input, allowing its user access to resources without passing through the normal security enforcement mechanism.

children.name: Logic/Time Bomb
children.description: The product contains code that is designed to disrupt the legitimate operation of the product (or its environment) when a certain time passes, or when a certain logical condition is met.

children.name: Spyware
children.description: The product collects personally identifiable information about a human user or the user's activities, but the product accesses this information using other resources besides itself, and it does not require that user's explicit approval or direct input into the product.

children.name: Suspicious Comment
children.description: The code contains comments that suggest the presence of bugs, incomplete functionality, or weaknesses.

children.name: Use of Hard-coded, Security-relevant Constants
children.description: The product uses hard-coded constants instead of symbolic names for security-critical values, which increases the likelihood of mistakes during code maintenance or security policy change.

children.name: Use of umask() with chmod-style Argument
children.description: The product calls umask() with an incorrect argument that is specified as if it is an argument to chmod().

children.name: Dead Code
children.description: The product contains dead code, which can never be executed.

children.name: Assignment to Variable without Use
children.description: The variable's value is assigned but never used, making it a dead store.

children.name: Expression is Always False
children.description: The product contains an expression that will always evaluate to false.

children.name: Expression is Always True
children.description: The product contains an expression that will always evaluate to true.

children.name: Improper Following of Specification by Caller
children.description: The product does not follow or incorrectly follows the specifications as required by the implementation language, environment, framework, protocol, or platform.

children.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of AWT Swing
children.description: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using AWT/Swing.

children.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Java I/O
children.description: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using the java.io package.

children.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Sockets
children.description: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using sockets.

children.name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Class Loader
children.description: The product violates the Enterprise JavaBeans (EJB) specification by using the class loader.

children.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Non-serializable Object Stored in Session
children.description: The product stores a non-serializable object as an HttpSession attribute, which can hurt reliability.

children.name: Object Model Violation: Just One of Equals and Hashcode Defined
children.description: The product does not maintain equal hashcodes for equal objects.

children.name: Empty Synchronized Block
children.description: The product contains an empty synchronized block.

children.name: Explicit Call to Finalize()
children.description: The product makes an explicit call to the finalize() method from outside the finalizer.

children.name: Call to Non-ubiquitous API
children.description: The product uses an API function that does not exist on all versions of the target platform. This could cause portability problems or inconsistencies that allow denial of service or other consequences.

children.name: J2EE Framework: Saving Unserializable Objects to Disk
children.description: When the J2EE container attempts to write unserializable objects to disk there is no guarantee that the process will complete successfully.

children.name: Multiple Binds to the Same Port
children.description: When multiple sockets are allowed to bind to the same port, other services on that port may be stolen or spoofed.

children.name: Function Call with Incorrectly Specified Arguments
children.description: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine with arguments that are not correctly specified, leading to always-incorrect behavior and resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Multiple Operations on Resource in Single-Operation Context
children.description: The product performs the same operation on a resource two or more times, when the operation should only be applied once.

children.name: Use of Potentially Dangerous Function
children.description: The product invokes a potentially dangerous function that could introduce a vulnerability if it is used incorrectly, but the function can also be used safely.

children.name: Function Call With Incorrect Order of Arguments
children.description: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies the arguments in an incorrect order, leading to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Incorrect Provision of Specified Functionality
children.description: The code does not function according to its published specifications, potentially leading to incorrect usage.

children.name: Function Call With Incorrect Number of Arguments
children.description: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies too many arguments, or too few arguments, which may lead to undefined behavior and resultant weaknesses.
