children.name: Function Call With Incorrect Argument Type
children.description: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies an argument that is the wrong data type, which may lead to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Function Call With Incorrectly Specified Argument Value
children.description: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies an argument that contains the wrong value, which may lead to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Function Call With Incorrect Variable or Reference as Argument
children.description: The product calls a function, procedure, or routine, but the caller specifies the wrong variable or reference as one of the arguments, which may lead to undefined behavior and resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Use of Low-Level Functionality
children.description: The product uses low-level functionality that is explicitly prohibited by the framework or specification under which the product is supposed to operate.

children.name: Improper Adherence to Coding Standards
children.description: The product does not follow certain coding rules for development, which can lead to resultant weaknesses or increase the severity of the associated vulnerabilities.

children.name: Reliance on Undefined, Unspecified, or Implementation-Defined Behavior
children.description: The product uses an API function, data structure, or other entity in a way that relies on properties that are not always guaranteed to hold for that entity.

children.name: Critical Data Element Declared Public
children.description: The product declares a critical variable, field, or member to be public when intended security policy requires it to be private.

children.name: Use of Path Manipulation Function without Maximum-sized Buffer
children.description: The product invokes a function for normalizing paths or file names, but it provides an output buffer that is smaller than the maximum possible size, such as PATH_MAX.

children.name: Hidden Functionality
children.description: The product contains functionality that is not documented, not part of the specification, and not accessible through an interface or command sequence that is obvious to the product's users or administrators.

children.name: Insufficient Visual Distinction of Homoglyphs Presented to User
children.description: The product displays information or identifiers to a user, but the display mechanism does not make it easy for the user to distinguish between visually similar or identical glyphs (homoglyphs), which may cause the user to misinterpret a glyph and perform an unintended, insecure action.

children.name: Use of Redundant Code
children.description:
The product has multiple functions, methods, procedures, macros, etc. that
					contain the same code.

children.name: Data Element Aggregating an Excessively Large Number of Non-Primitive Elements
children.description:
The product uses a data element that has an excessively large
					number of sub-elements with non-primitive data types such as structures or aggregated objects.

children.name: Architecture with Number of Horizontal Layers Outside of Expected Range
children.description:
The product's architecture contains too many - or too few -
					horizontal layers.

children.name: Parent Class with a Virtual Destructor and a Child Class without a Virtual Destructor
children.description: A parent class has a virtual destructor method, but the parent has a child class that does not have a virtual destructor.

children.name: Modules with Circular Dependencies
children.description: The product contains modules in which one module has references that cycle back to itself, i.e., there are circular dependencies.

children.name: Invokable Control Element with Large Number of Outward Calls
children.description:
The code contains callable control elements that
         contain an excessively large number of references to other
         application objects external to the context of the callable,
         i.e. a Fan-Out value that is excessively large.

children.name: Missing Documentation for Design
children.description: The product does not have documentation that represents how it is designed.

children.name: Invocation of a Control Element at an Unnecessarily Deep Horizontal Layer
children.description:
The code at one architectural layer invokes code that resides
					at a deeper layer than the adjacent layer, i.e., the invocation skips at least one
					layer, and the invoked code is not part of a vertical utility layer that can be referenced from any horizontal layer.

children.name: Multiple Inheritance from Concrete Classes
children.description:
The product contains a class with inheritance from more than
					one concrete class.

children.name: Invokable Control Element with Variadic Parameters
children.description:
A named-callable or method control element has a signature that
					supports a variable (variadic) number of parameters or arguments.

children.name: Data Access Operations Outside of Expected Data Manager Component
children.description: The product uses a dedicated, central data manager component as required by design, but it contains code that performs data-access operations that do not use this data manager.

children.name: Insufficient Technical Documentation
children.description:
The product does not contain sufficient
         technical or engineering documentation (whether on paper or
         in electronic form) that contains descriptions of all the
         relevant software/hardware elements of the product, such as
         its usage, structure, architectural components

children.name: Excessive Number of Inefficient Server-Side Data Accesses
children.description: The product performs too many data queries without using efficient data processing functionality such as stored procedures.

children.name: Insufficient Encapsulation
children.description: The product does not sufficiently hide the internal representation and implementation details of data or methods, which might allow external components or modules to modify data unexpectedly, invoke unexpected functionality, or introduce dependencies that the programmer did not intend.

children.name: Parent Class with References to Child Class
children.description: The code has a parent class that contains references to a child class, its methods, or its members.

children.name: Invokable Control Element with Signature Containing an Excessive Number of Parameters
children.description:
The product contains a function, subroutine, or method whose signature has an unnecessarily large number of
					parameters/arguments.

children.name: Runtime Resource Management Control Element in a Component Built to Run on Application Servers
children.description: The product uses deployed components from application servers, but it also uses low-level functions/methods for management of resources, instead of the API provided by the application server.

children.name: Missing Serialization Control Element
children.description:
The product contains a serializable data element that does not
					have an associated serialization method.

children.name: Inconsistency Between Implementation and Documented Design
children.description:
The implementation of the product is not consistent with the
					design as described within the relevant documentation.

children.name: Empty Exception Block
children.description: An invokable code block contains an exception handling block that does not contain any code, i.e. is empty.

children.name: Serializable Data Element Containing non-Serializable Item Elements
children.description:
The product contains a serializable, storable data element such as a field or member,
					but the data element contains member elements that are not
					serializable.

children.name: Empty Code Block
children.description: The source code contains a block that does not contain any code, i.e., the block is empty.

children.name: Class with Excessively Deep Inheritance
children.description:
A class has an inheritance level that is too high, i.e., it
					has a large number of parent classes.

children.name: Unconditional Control Flow Transfer outside of Switch Block
children.description:
The product performs unconditional control transfer (such as a
					"goto") in code outside of a branching structure such as a switch
					block.

children.name: Insufficient Adherence to Expected Conventions
children.description:
The product's architecture, source code, design, documentation,
					or other artifact does not follow required conventions.

children.name: Inappropriate Source Code Style or Formatting
children.description:
The source code does not follow
				desired style or formatting for indentation, white
				space, comments, etc.

children.name: Parent Class without Virtual Destructor Method
children.description: A parent class contains one or more child classes, but the parent class does not have a virtual destructor method.

children.name: Source Code File with Excessive Number of Lines of Code
children.description:
A source code file has too many lines of
					code.

children.name: Class Instance Self Destruction Control Element
children.description: The code contains a class instance that calls the method or function to delete or destroy itself.

children.name: Data Access from Outside Expected Data Manager Component
children.description: The product is intended to manage data access through a particular data manager component such as a relational or non-SQL database, but it contains code that performs data access operations without using that component.

children.name: Invokable Control Element with Excessive Volume of Commented-out Code
children.description:
A function, method, procedure, etc. contains an excessive amount of code that has been
					commented out within its body.

children.name: Class with Excessive Number of Child Classes
children.description:
A class contains an unnecessarily large number of
					children.

children.name: Class with Virtual Method without a Virtual Destructor
children.description: A class contains a virtual method, but the method does not have an associated virtual destructor.

children.name: Method Containing Access of a Member Element from Another Class
children.description:
A method for a class performs an operation that directly
					accesses a member element from another class.

children.name: Use of Same Invokable Control Element in Multiple Architectural Layers
children.description:
The product uses the same control element across multiple
					architectural layers.

children.name: Excessively Complex Data Representation
children.description: The product uses an unnecessarily complex internal representation for its data structures or interrelationships between those structures.

children.name: Loop Condition Value Update within the Loop
children.description:
The product uses a loop with a control flow condition based on
					a value that is updated within the body of the loop.

children.name: Persistent Storable Data Element without Associated Comparison Control Element
children.description:
The product uses a storable data element that does not have
					all of the associated functions or methods that are necessary to support
					comparison.

children.name: Data Element containing Pointer Item without Proper Copy Control Element
children.description: The code contains a data element with a pointer that does not have an associated copy or constructor method.

children.name: Inconsistent Naming Conventions for Identifiers
children.description:
The product's code, documentation, or other artifacts do not
					consistently use the same naming conventions for variables, callables, groups of
					related callables, I/O capabilities, data types, file names, or similar types of
					elements.

children.name: Insufficient Isolation of System-Dependent Functions
children.description:
The product or code does not isolate system-dependent
					functionality into separate standalone modules.

children.name: Reliance on Runtime Component in Generated Code
children.description:
The product uses automatically-generated code that cannot be
					executed without a specific runtime support component.

children.name: Reliance on Machine-Dependent Data Representation
children.description:
The code uses a data representation that relies on low-level
					data representation or constructs that may vary across different processors,
					physical machines, OSes, or other physical components.

children.name: Use of Platform-Dependent Third Party Components
children.description:
The product relies on third-party components that do
					not provide equivalent functionality across all desirable
					platforms.

children.name: Insufficient Encapsulation of Machine-Dependent Functionality
children.description:
The product or code uses machine-dependent functionality, but
					it does not sufficiently encapsulate or isolate this functionality from
					the rest of the code.

children.name: Insufficient Use of Symbolic Constants
children.description:
The source code uses literal constants that may need to change
					or evolve over time, instead of using symbolic constants.

children.name: Insufficient Isolation of Symbolic Constant Definitions
children.description:
The source code uses symbolic constants, but it does not
					sufficiently place the definitions of these constants into a more centralized or
					isolated location.

children.name: Excessive Reliance on Global Variables
children.description:
The code is structured in a way that relies too much on using
					or setting global variables throughout various points in the code, instead of
					preserving the associated information in a narrower, more local
					context.

children.name: Use of Same Variable for Multiple Purposes
children.description:
The code contains a callable, block, or other code element in
					which the same variable is used to control more than one unique task or store
					more than one instance of data.

children.name: Incomplete Design Documentation
children.description:
The product's design documentation does not adequately describe
					control flow, data flow, system initialization, relationships between tasks,
					components, rationales, or other important aspects of the
					design.

children.name: Incomplete I/O Documentation
children.description:
The product's documentation does not adequately define inputs,
					outputs, or system/software interfaces.

children.name: Incomplete Documentation of Program Execution
children.description:
The document does not fully define all mechanisms that are used
					to control or influence how product-specific programs are
					executed.

children.name: Inappropriate Comment Style
children.description:
The source code uses comment styles or formats that are
					inconsistent or do not follow expected standards for the
					product.

children.name: Inappropriate Whitespace Style
children.description:
The source code contains whitespace that is inconsistent across
					the code or does not follow expected standards for the
					product.

children.name: Source Code Element without Standard Prologue
children.description:
The source code contains elements such as source files 
					that do not consistently provide a prologue or header that has been
					standardized for the project.

children.name: Inaccurate Source Code Comments
children.description:
The source code contains comments that do not accurately
					describe or explain aspects of the portion of the code with which the comment is
					associated.

children.name: Callable with Insufficient Behavioral Summary
children.description:
The code contains a function or method whose signature and/or associated
					inline documentation does not sufficiently describe the callable's inputs, outputs,
					side effects, assumptions, or return codes.

children.name: Insufficient Documentation of Error Handling Techniques
children.description:
The documentation does not sufficiently describe the techniques
					that are used for error handling, exception processing, or similar
					mechanisms.

children.name: Excessive Use of Unconditional Branching
children.description:
The code uses too many unconditional branches (such as
					"goto").

children.name: Excessive Code Complexity
children.description:
The code is too complex, as calculated using a well-defined,
					quantitative measure.

children.name: Excessive McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity
children.description:
The code contains McCabe cyclomatic complexity that exceeds a
	desirable maximum.

children.name: Excessive Halstead Complexity
children.description:
The code is structured in a way that a Halstead complexity
					measure exceeds a desirable maximum.

children.name: Excessive Use of Self-Modifying Code
children.description: The product uses too much self-modifying code.

children.name: Excessively Deep Nesting
children.description:
The code contains a callable or other code grouping in which
					the nesting / branching is too deep.

children.name: Excessive Attack Surface
children.description:
The product has an attack surface whose quantitative
					measurement exceeds a desirable maximum.

children.name: Declaration of Variable with Unnecessarily Wide Scope
children.description:
The source code declares a variable in one scope, but the
					variable is only used within a narrower scope.

children.name: Compilation with Insufficient Warnings or Errors
children.description:
The code is compiled without sufficient warnings enabled, which
					may prevent the detection of subtle bugs or quality
					issues.

children.name: Irrelevant Code
children.description:
The product contains code that is not essential for execution,
	     i.e. makes no state changes and has no side effects that alter
	     data or control flow, such that removal of the code would have no impact
	     to functionality or correctness.

children.name: Use of Prohibited Code
children.description:
The product uses a function, library, or third party component
	     that has been explicitly prohibited, whether by the developer or
	     the customer.

children.name: Failure to Disable Reserved Bits
children.description: The reserved bits in a hardware design are not disabled prior to production. Typically, reserved bits are used for future capabilities and should not support any functional logic in the design.   However, designers might covertly use these bits to debug or further develop new capabilities in product

children.name: Improper Finite State Machines (FSMs) in Hardware Logic
children.description: Faulty finite state machines (FSMs) in the hardware logic allow an attacker to put the system in an undefined state, to cause a denial of service (DoS) or gain privileges on the victim's system.

children.name: Multiple Releases of Same Resource or Handle
children.description: The product attempts to close or release a resource or handle more than once, without any successful open between the close operations.

children.name: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component
children.description: The product is built from multiple separate components, but it uses a component that is not sufficiently trusted to meet expectations for security, reliability, updateability, and maintainability.

children.name: Insecure Setting of Generative AI/ML Model Inference Parameters
children.description:
The product has a component that relies on a
	  generative AI/ML model configured with inference parameters that
	  produce an unacceptably high rate of erroneous or unexpected
	  outputs.

children.name: Protection Mechanism Failure
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to protection mechanism failure.

children.name: Collapse of Data into Unsafe Value
children.description: The product filters data in a way that causes it to be reduced or "collapsed" into an unsafe value that violates an expected security property.

children.name: Incomplete List of Disallowed Inputs
children.description: The product implements a protection mechanism that relies on a list of inputs (or properties of inputs) that are not allowed by policy or otherwise require other action to neutralize before additional processing takes place, but the list is incomplete.

children.name: Truncation of Security-relevant Information
children.description: The product truncates the display, recording, or processing of security-relevant information in a way that can obscure the source or nature of an attack.

children.name: Omission of Security-relevant Information
children.description: The product does not record or display information that would be important for identifying the source or nature of an attack, or determining if an action is safe.

children.name: Obscured Security-relevant Information by Alternate Name
children.description: The product records security-relevant information according to an alternate name of the affected entity, instead of the canonical name.

children.name: Product UI does not Warn User of Unsafe Actions
children.description: The product's user interface does not warn the user before undertaking an unsafe action on behalf of that user. This makes it easier for attackers to trick users into inflicting damage to their system.

children.name: Insufficient UI Warning of Dangerous Operations
children.description: The user interface provides a warning to a user regarding dangerous or sensitive operations, but the warning is not noticeable enough to warrant attention.

children.name: Multiple Interpretations of UI Input
children.description: The UI has multiple interpretations of user input but does not prompt the user when it selects the less secure interpretation.

children.name: Client-Side Enforcement of Server-Side Security
children.description: The product is composed of a server that relies on the client to implement a mechanism that is intended to protect the server.

children.name: Protection Mechanism Failure
children.description: The product does not use or incorrectly uses a protection mechanism that provides sufficient defense against directed attacks against the product.

children.name: Selection of Less-Secure Algorithm During Negotiation ('Algorithm Downgrade')
children.description: A protocol or its implementation supports interaction between multiple actors and allows those actors to negotiate which algorithm should be used as a protection mechanism such as encryption or authentication, but it does not select the strongest algorithm that is available to both parties.

children.name: Insufficient Logging
children.description: When a security-critical event occurs, the product either does not record the event or omits important details about the event when logging it.

children.name: Reliance on Untrusted Inputs in a Security Decision
children.description: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of an input, but the input can be modified by an untrusted actor in a way that bypasses the protection mechanism.

children.name: Inadequate Detection or Handling of Adversarial Input Perturbations in Automated Recognition Mechanism
children.description: The product uses an automated mechanism such as machine learning to recognize complex data inputs (e.g. image or audio) as a particular concept or category, but it does not properly detect or handle inputs that have been modified or constructed in a way that causes the mechanism to detect a differen

children.name: Semiconductor Defects in Hardware Logic with Security-Sensitive Implications
children.description: The security-sensitive hardware module contains semiconductor defects.

children.name: Incorrect Selection of Fuse Values
children.description: The logic level used to set a system to a secure state relies on a fuse being unblown.

children.name: Product Released in Non-Release Configuration
children.description: The product released to market is released in pre-production or manufacturing configuration.

children.name: Missing Protection Against Hardware Reverse Engineering Using Integrated Circuit (IC) Imaging Techniques
children.description: Information stored in hardware may be recovered by an attacker with the capability to capture and analyze images of the integrated circuit using techniques such as scanning electron microscopy.

children.name: Public Key Re-Use for Signing both Debug and Production Code
children.description: The same public key is used for signing both debug and production code.

children.name: Missing Support for Security Features in On-chip Fabrics or Buses
children.description: On-chip fabrics or buses either do not support or are not configured to support privilege separation or other security features, such as access control.

children.name: Improper Protection against Electromagnetic Fault Injection (EM-FI)
children.description: The device is susceptible to electromagnetic fault injection attacks, causing device internal information to be compromised or security mechanisms to be bypassed.

children.name: Missing Immutable Root of Trust in Hardware
children.description: A missing immutable root of trust in the hardware results in the ability to bypass secure boot or execute untrusted or adversarial boot code.

children.name: Improper Protections Against Hardware Overheating
children.description: A hardware device is missing or has inadequate protection features to prevent overheating.

children.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
children.description:
The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations
			in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as
			the timely detection of failures or attacks.

children.name: Randomness
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to randomness.

children.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Insufficient Session-ID Length
children.description: The J2EE application is configured to use an insufficient session ID length.

children.name: Reusing a Nonce, Key Pair in Encryption
children.description: Nonces should be used for the present occasion and only once.

children.name: Generation of Predictable IV with CBC Mode
children.description: The product generates and uses a predictable initialization Vector (IV) with Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) Mode, which causes algorithms to be susceptible to dictionary attacks when they are encrypted under the same key.

children.name: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
children.description: The product uses insufficiently random numbers or values in a security context that depends on unpredictable numbers.

children.name: Insufficient Entropy
children.description: The product uses an algorithm or scheme that produces insufficient entropy, leaving patterns or clusters of values that are more likely to occur than others.

children.name: Insufficient Entropy in PRNG
children.description: The lack of entropy available for, or used by, a Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) can be a stability and security threat.

children.name: Improper Handling of Insufficient Entropy in TRNG
children.description: True random number generators (TRNG) generally have a limited source of entropy and therefore can fail or block.

children.name: Small Space of Random Values
children.description: The number of possible random values is smaller than needed by the product, making it more susceptible to brute force attacks.

children.name: Incorrect Usage of Seeds in Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
children.description: The product uses a Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) but does not correctly manage seeds.

children.name: Same Seed in Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
children.description: A Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) uses the same seed each time the product is initialized.

children.name: Predictable Seed in Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
children.description: A Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) is initialized from a predictable seed, such as the process ID or system time.

children.name: Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
children.description: The product uses a Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) in a security context, but the PRNG's algorithm is not cryptographically strong.

children.name: Small Seed Space in PRNG
children.description: A Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) uses a relatively small seed space, which makes it more susceptible to brute force attacks.

children.name: Generation of Predictable Numbers or Identifiers
children.description: The product uses a scheme that generates numbers or identifiers that are more predictable than required.

children.name: Predictable from Observable State
children.description: A number or object is predictable based on observations that the attacker can make about the state of the system or network, such as time, process ID, etc.

children.name: Predictable Exact Value from Previous Values
children.description: An exact value or random number can be precisely predicted by observing previous values.

children.name: Predictable Value Range from Previous Values
children.description: The product's random number generator produces a series of values which, when observed, can be used to infer a relatively small range of possibilities for the next value that could be generated.

children.name: Use of Invariant Value in Dynamically Changing Context
children.description: The product uses a constant value, name, or reference, but this value can (or should) vary across different environments.

children.name: Generation of Weak Initialization Vector (IV)
children.description:
The product uses a cryptographic primitive that uses an Initialization
			Vector (IV), but the product does not generate IVs that are
			sufficiently unpredictable or unique according to the expected
			cryptographic requirements for that primitive.

children.name: Use of Predictable Algorithm in Random Number Generator
children.description: The device uses an algorithm that is predictable and generates a pseudo-random number.

children.name: Resource Control
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to resource control.

children.name: Covert Timing Channel
children.description: Covert timing channels convey information by modulating some aspect of system behavior over time, so that the program receiving the information can observe system behavior and infer protected information.

children.name: Use of Externally-Controlled Input to Select Classes or Code ('Unsafe Reflection')
children.description: The product uses external input with reflection to select which classes or code to use, but it does not sufficiently prevent the input from selecting improper classes or code.

children.name: PHP External Variable Modification
children.description: A PHP application does not properly protect against the modification of variables from external sources, such as query parameters or cookies. This can expose the application to numerous weaknesses that would not exist otherwise.

children.name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
children.description: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently ensuring that the resulting data will be valid.

children.name: Covert Channel
children.description: A covert channel is a path that can be used to transfer information in a way not intended by the system's designers.

children.name: Covert Storage Channel
children.description: A covert storage channel transfers information through the setting of bits by one program and the reading of those bits by another. What distinguishes this case from that of ordinary operation is that the bits are used to convey encoded information.

children.name: Operation on a Resource after Expiration or Release
children.description: The product uses, accesses, or otherwise operates on a resource after that resource has been expired, released, or revoked.

children.name: Premature Release of Resource During Expected Lifetime
children.description: The product releases a resource that is still intended to be used by itself or another actor.

children.name: Use of Expired File Descriptor
children.description: The product uses or accesses a file descriptor after it has been closed.

children.name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies multiple attributes, properties, or fields that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control which attributes can be modified.

children.name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
children.description:
The product relies on third-party components that are not
					actively supported or maintained by the original developer or a trusted proxy
					for the original developer.

children.name: Application-Level Admin Tool with Inconsistent View of Underlying Operating System
children.description: The product provides an application for administrators to manage parts of the underlying operating system, but the application does not accurately identify all of the relevant entities or resources that exist in the OS; that is, the application's model of the OS's state is inconsistent with the OS's

children.name: Mirrored Regions with Different Values
children.description: The product's architecture mirrors regions without ensuring that their contents always stay in sync.

children.name: Firmware Not Updateable
children.description:
The product does not provide its
			users with the ability to update or patch its
			firmware to address any vulnerabilities or
			weaknesses that may be present.

children.name: Missing Ability to Patch ROM Code
children.description: Missing an ability to patch ROM code may leave a System or System-on-Chip (SoC) in a vulnerable state.

children.name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes ('Prototype Pollution')
children.description: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies attributes that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control modifications of attributes of the object prototype.

children.name: Reliance on Component That is Not Updateable
children.description: The product contains a component that cannot be updated or patched in order to remove vulnerabilities or significant bugs.

children.name: Resource Lifecycle Management
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to resource lifecycle management.

children.name: Improper Control of Filename for Include/Require Statement in PHP Program ('PHP Remote File Inclusion')
children.description: The PHP application receives input from an upstream component, but it does not restrict or incorrectly restricts the input before its usage in "require," "include," or similar functions.

children.name: Incorrect Access of Indexable Resource ('Range Error')
children.description: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts operations within the boundaries of a resource that is accessed using an index or pointer, such as memory or files.

children.name: Improper Handling of Case Sensitivity
children.description: The product does not properly account for differences in case sensitivity when accessing or determining the properties of a resource, leading to inconsistent results.

children.name: Integer Coercion Error
children.description: Integer coercion refers to a set of flaws pertaining to the type casting, extension, or truncation of primitive data types.

children.name: Unexpected Sign Extension
children.description: The product performs an operation on a number that causes it to be sign extended when it is transformed into a larger data type. When the original number is negative, this can produce unexpected values that lead to resultant weaknesses.

children.name: Signed to Unsigned Conversion Error
children.description: The product uses a signed primitive and performs a cast to an unsigned primitive, which can produce an unexpected value if the value of the signed primitive can not be represented using an unsigned primitive.

children.name: Unsigned to Signed Conversion Error
children.description: The product uses an unsigned primitive and performs a cast to a signed primitive, which can produce an unexpected value if the value of the unsigned primitive can not be represented using a signed primitive.

children.name: Numeric Truncation Error
children.description: Truncation errors occur when a primitive is cast to a primitive of a smaller size and data is lost in the conversion.

children.name: Improper Removal of Sensitive Information Before Storage or Transfer
children.description: The product stores, transfers, or shares a resource that contains sensitive information, but it does not properly remove that information before the product makes the resource available to unauthorized actors.

children.name: Information Loss or Omission
children.description: The product does not record, or improperly records, security-relevant information that leads to an incorrect decision or hampers later analysis.

children.name: Sensitive Information in Resource Not Removed Before Reuse
children.description: The product releases a resource such as memory or a file so that it can be made available for reuse, but it does not clear or "zeroize" the information contained in the resource before the product performs a critical state transition or makes the resource available for reuse by other entities.

children.name: Creation of chroot Jail Without Changing Working Directory
children.description: The product uses the chroot() system call to create a jail, but does not change the working directory afterward. This does not prevent access to files outside of the jail.

children.name: Incomplete Internal State Distinction
children.description: The product does not properly determine which state it is in, causing it to assume it is in state X when in fact it is in state Y, causing it to perform incorrect operations in a security-relevant manner.

children.name: Symbolic Name not Mapping to Correct Object
children.description: A constant symbolic reference to an object is used, even though the reference can resolve to a different object over time.

children.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
children.description: The product does not properly control the allocation and maintenance of a limited resource.

children.name: Improper Resource Shutdown or Release
children.description: The product does not release or incorrectly releases a resource before it is made available for re-use.

children.name: Asymmetric Resource Consumption (Amplification)
children.description: The product does not properly control situations in which an adversary can cause the product to consume or produce excessive resources without requiring the adversary to invest equivalent work or otherwise prove authorization, i.e., the adversary's influence is "asymmetric."

children.name: Insufficient Control of Network Message Volume (Network Amplification)
children.description: The product does not sufficiently monitor or control transmitted network traffic volume, so that an actor can cause the product to transmit more traffic than should be allowed for that actor.

children.name: Inefficient Algorithmic Complexity
children.description: An algorithm in a product has an inefficient worst-case computational complexity that may be detrimental to system performance and can be triggered by an attacker, typically using crafted manipulations that ensure that the worst case is being reached.

children.name: Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)
children.description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles a compressed input with a very high compression ratio that produces a large output.

children.name: Insufficient Resource Pool
children.description: The product's resource pool is not large enough to handle peak demand, which allows an attacker to prevent others from accessing the resource by using a (relatively) large number of requests for resources.

children.name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
children.description: The product allows the upload or transfer of dangerous file types that are automatically processed within its environment.

children.name: Insecure Default Variable Initialization
children.description: The product, by default, initializes an internal variable with an insecure or less secure value than is possible.

children.name: External Initialization of Trusted Variables or Data Stores
children.description: The product initializes critical internal variables or data stores using inputs that can be modified by untrusted actors.

children.name: Missing Initialization of a Variable
children.description: The product does not initialize critical variables, which causes the execution environment to use unexpected values.

children.name: Use of Uninitialized Variable
children.description: The code uses a variable that has not been initialized, leading to unpredictable or unintended results.

children.name: Incomplete Cleanup
children.description: The product does not properly "clean up" and remove temporary or supporting resources after they have been used.

children.name: Improper Cleanup on Thrown Exception
children.description: The product does not clean up its state or incorrectly cleans up its state when an exception is thrown, leading to unexpected state or control flow.

children.name: Modification of Assumed-Immutable Data (MAID)
children.description: The product does not properly protect an assumed-immutable element from being modified by an attacker.

children.name: Reliance on Package-level Scope
children.description: Java packages are not inherently closed; therefore, relying on them for code security is not a good practice.

children.name: Private Data Structure Returned From A Public Method
children.description: The product has a method that is declared public, but returns a reference to a private data structure, which could then be modified in unexpected ways.

children.name: Public Data Assigned to Private Array-Typed Field
children.description: Assigning public data to a private array is equivalent to giving public access to the array.

children.name: Trust Boundary Violation
children.description: The product mixes trusted and untrusted data in the same data structure or structured message.

children.name: finalize() Method Without super.finalize()
children.description: The product contains a finalize() method that does not call super.finalize().

children.name: clone() Method Without super.clone()
children.description: The product contains a clone() method that does not call super.clone() to obtain the new object.

children.name: Attempt to Access Child of a Non-structure Pointer
children.description: Casting a non-structure type to a structure type and accessing a field can lead to memory access errors or data corruption.

children.name: Public Static Final Field References Mutable Object
children.description: A public or protected static final field references a mutable object, which allows the object to be changed by malicious code, or accidentally from another package.

children.name: Externally Controlled Reference to a Resource in Another Sphere
children.description: The product uses an externally controlled name or reference that resolves to a resource that is outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Exposed Unsafe ActiveX Method
children.description: An ActiveX control is intended for use in a web browser, but it exposes dangerous methods that perform actions that are outside of the browser's security model (e.g. the zone or domain).

children.name: Improper Synchronization
children.description: The product utilizes multiple threads, processes, components, or systems to allow temporary access to a shared resource that can only be exclusive to one process at a time, but it does not properly synchronize these actions, which might cause simultaneous accesses of this resource by multiple thread

children.name: Improper Control of a Resource Through its Lifetime
children.description: The product does not maintain or incorrectly maintains control over a resource throughout its lifetime of creation, use, and release.

children.name: Improper Initialization
children.description: The product does not initialize or incorrectly initializes a resource, which might leave the resource in an unexpected state when it is accessed or used.

children.name: Operation on Resource in Wrong Phase of Lifetime
children.description: The product performs an operation on a resource at the wrong phase of the resource's lifecycle, which can lead to unexpected behaviors.

children.name: Incorrect Resource Transfer Between Spheres
children.description: The product does not properly transfer a resource/behavior to another sphere, or improperly imports a resource/behavior from another sphere, in a manner that provides unintended control over that resource.

children.name: External Influence of Sphere Definition
children.description: The product does not prevent the definition of control spheres from external actors.

children.name: Incorrect Conversion between Numeric Types
children.description: When converting from one data type to another, such as long to integer, data can be omitted or translated in a way that produces unexpected values. If the resulting values are used in a sensitive context, then dangerous behaviors may occur.

children.name: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast
children.description: The product does not correctly convert an object, resource, or structure from one type to a different type.

children.name: Use of Incorrectly-Resolved Name or Reference
children.description: The product uses a name or reference to access a resource, but the name/reference resolves to a resource that is outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Exposed Dangerous Method or Function
children.description: The product provides an Applications Programming Interface (API) or similar interface for interaction with external actors, but the interface includes a dangerous method or function that is not properly restricted.

children.name: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
children.description: The product allocates a reusable resource or group of resources on behalf of an actor without imposing any intended restrictions on the size or number of resources that can be allocated.

children.name: Missing Reference to Active Allocated Resource
children.description: The product does not properly maintain a reference to a resource that has been allocated, which prevents the resource from being reclaimed.

children.name: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime
children.description: The product does not release a resource after its effective lifetime has ended, i.e., after the resource is no longer needed.

children.name: Missing Reference to Active File Descriptor or Handle
children.description: The product does not properly maintain references to a file descriptor or handle, which prevents that file descriptor/handle from being reclaimed.

children.name: Allocation of File Descriptors or Handles Without Limits or Throttling
children.description: The product allocates file descriptors or handles on behalf of an actor without imposing any restrictions on how many descriptors can be allocated, in violation of the intended security policy for that actor.

children.name: Missing Release of File Descriptor or Handle after Effective Lifetime
children.description: The product does not release a file descriptor or handle after its effective lifetime has ended, i.e., after the file descriptor/handle is no longer needed.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Recursive Entity References in DTDs ('XML Entity Expansion')
children.description: The product uses XML documents and allows their structure to be defined with a Document Type Definition (DTD), but it does not properly control the number of recursive definitions of entities.

children.name: Logging of Excessive Data
children.description: The product logs too much information, making log files hard to process and possibly hindering recovery efforts or forensic analysis after an attack.

children.name: Exposed IOCTL with Insufficient Access Control
children.description: The product implements an IOCTL with functionality that should be restricted, but it does not properly enforce access control for the IOCTL.

children.name: Improper Control of Document Type Definition
children.description: The product does not restrict a reference to a Document Type Definition (DTD) to the intended control sphere. This might allow attackers to reference arbitrary DTDs, possibly causing the product to expose files, consume excessive system resources, or execute arbitrary http requests on behalf of the

children.name: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
children.description: The product imports, requires, or includes executable functionality (such as a library) from a source that is outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Inclusion of Web Functionality from an Untrusted Source
children.description: The product includes web functionality (such as a web widget) from another domain, which causes it to operate within the domain of the product, potentially granting total access and control of the product to the untrusted source.

children.name: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type ('Type Confusion')
children.description: The product allocates or initializes a resource such as a pointer, object, or variable using one type, but it later accesses that resource using a type that is incompatible with the original type.

children.name: Use of Uninitialized Resource
children.description: The product uses or accesses a resource that has not been initialized.

children.name: Missing Initialization of Resource
children.description: The product does not initialize a critical resource.

children.name: Improper Update of Reference Count
children.description: The product uses a reference count to manage a resource, but it does not update or incorrectly updates the reference count.

children.name: Improper Control of Dynamically-Managed Code Resources
children.description: The product does not properly restrict reading from or writing to dynamically-managed code resources such as variables, objects, classes, attributes, functions, or executable instructions or statements.

children.name: Improper Restriction of Power Consumption
children.description: The product operates in an environment in which power is a limited resource that cannot be automatically replenished, but the product does not properly restrict the amount of power that its operation consumes.

children.name: Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information
children.description: The product stores sensitive information without properly limiting read or write access by unauthorized actors.

children.name: Static Member Data Element outside of a Singleton Class Element
children.description:
The code contains a member element that is declared as static (but not final), in which
					its parent class element 
					is not a singleton class - that is, a class element that can be used only once in
					the 'to' association of a Create action.

children.name: Creation of Immutable Text Using String Concatenation
children.description: The product creates an immutable text string using string concatenation operations.

children.name: Excessive Data Query Operations in a Large Data Table
children.description:
The product performs a data query with a large number of joins
					and sub-queries on a large data table.

children.name: Excessive Platform Resource Consumption within a Loop
children.description:
The product has a loop body or loop condition that contains a control element that directly or
					indirectly consumes platform resources, e.g. messaging, sessions, locks, or file
					descriptors.

children.name: Initialization with Hard-Coded Network Resource Configuration Data
children.description: The product initializes data using hard-coded values that act as network resource identifiers.

children.name: Excessive Use of Hard-Coded Literals in Initialization
children.description:
The product initializes a data element using a hard-coded
					literal that is not a simple integer or static constant element.

children.name: Creation of Class Instance within a Static Code Block
children.description: A static code block creates an instance of a class.

children.name: Excessive Execution of Sequential Searches of Data Resource
children.description:
The product contains a data query against an SQL table or view
					that is configured in a way that does not utilize an index and may cause
					sequential searches to be performed.

children.name: Data Resource Access without Use of Connection Pooling
children.description:
The product accesses a data resource through a database without using a
					connection pooling capability.

children.name: Non-SQL Invokable Control Element with Excessive Number of Data Resource Accesses
children.description: The product contains a client with a function or method that contains a large number of data accesses/queries that are sent through a data manager, i.e., does not use efficient database capabilities.

children.name: Invokable Control Element with Excessive File or Data Access Operations
children.description:
A function or method contains too many
					operations that utilize a data manager or file resource.

children.name: Large Data Table with Excessive Number of Indices
children.description:
The product uses a large data table that contains an excessively large number of
					indices.

children.name: Use of Object without Invoking Destructor Method
children.description:
The product contains a method that accesses an object but does not later invoke
					the element's associated finalize/destructor method.

children.name: Excessive Index Range Scan for a Data Resource
children.description:
The product contains an index range scan for a large data table,
					but the scan can cover a large number of rows.

children.name: Inefficient CPU Computation
children.description:
The product performs CPU computations using
         algorithms that are not as efficient as they could be for the
         needs of the developer, i.e., the computations can be
         optimized further.

children.name: Initialization of a Resource with an Insecure Default
children.description: The product initializes or sets a resource with a default that is intended to be changed by the product's installer, administrator, or maintainer, but the default is not secure.

children.name: Incorrect Register Defaults or Module Parameters
children.description: Hardware description language code incorrectly defines register defaults or hardware Intellectual Property (IP) parameters to insecure values.

children.name: Creation of Emergent Resource
children.description: The product manages resources or behaves in a way that indirectly creates a new, distinct resource that can be used by attackers in violation of the intended policy.

children.name: Incorrect Use of Autoboxing and Unboxing for Performance Critical Operations
children.description: The code uses boxed primitives, which may introduce inefficiencies into performance-critical operations.

children.name: Improper Zeroization of Hardware Register
children.description: The hardware product does not properly clear sensitive information from built-in registers when the user of the hardware block changes.

children.name: Improper Write Handling in Limited-write Non-Volatile Memories
children.description: The product does not implement or incorrectly implements wear leveling operations in limited-write non-volatile memories.

children.name: Improper Preservation of Consistency Between Independent Representations of Shared State
children.description: The product has or supports multiple distributed components or sub-systems that are each required to keep their own local copy of shared data - such as state or cache - but the product does not ensure that all local copies remain consistent with each other.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive System Information Due to Uncleared Debug Information
children.description: The hardware does not fully clear security-sensitive values, such as keys and intermediate values in cryptographic operations, when debug mode is entered.

children.name: Improper Scrubbing of Sensitive Data from Decommissioned Device
children.description: The product does not properly provide a capability for the product administrator to remove sensitive data at the time the product is decommissioned.  A scrubbing capability could be missing, insufficient, or incorrect.

children.name: Uninitialized Value on Reset for Registers Holding Security Settings
children.description: Security-critical logic is not set to a known value on reset.

children.name: Sensitive Information Uncleared Before Debug/Power State Transition
children.description: The product performs a power or debug state transition, but it does not clear sensitive information that should no longer be accessible due to changes to information access restrictions.

children.name: Cryptographic Operations are run Before Supporting Units are Ready
children.description: Performing cryptographic operations without ensuring that the supporting inputs are ready to supply valid data may compromise the cryptographic result.

children.name: Insufficient or Incomplete Data Removal within Hardware Component
children.description: The product's data removal process does not completely delete all data and potentially sensitive information within hardware components.

children.name: Improperly Controlled Sequential Memory Allocation
children.description: The product manages a group of objects or resources and performs a separate memory allocation for each object, but it does not properly limit the total amount of memory that is consumed by all of the combined objects.

children.name: Remanent Data Readable after Memory Erase
children.description: Confidential information stored in memory circuits is readable or recoverable after being cleared or erased.

children.name: Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity
children.description: The product uses a regular expression with an inefficient, possibly exponential worst-case computational complexity that consumes excessive CPU cycles.

children.name: Information Exposure through Microarchitectural State after Transient Execution
children.description: The processor does not properly clear microarchitectural state after incorrect microcode assists or speculative execution, resulting in transient execution.

children.name: Insecure Operation on Windows Junction / Mount Point
children.description: The product opens a file or directory, but it does not properly prevent the name from being associated with a junction or mount point to a destination that is outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Incorrect Parsing of Numbers with Different Radices
children.description: The product parses numeric input assuming base 10 (decimal) values, but it does not account for inputs that use a different base number (radix).

children.name: Incorrect Initialization of Resource
children.description: The product attempts to initialize a resource but does not correctly do so, which might leave the resource in an unexpected, incorrect, or insecure state when it is accessed.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information during Transient Execution
children.description: A processor event or prediction may allow incorrect operations (or correct operations with incorrect data) to execute transiently, potentially exposing data over a covert channel.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information in Shared Microarchitectural Structures during Transient Execution
children.description:
A processor event may allow transient operations to access
			architecturally restricted data (for example, in another address
			space) in a shared microarchitectural structure (for example, a CPU
			cache), potentially exposing the data over a covert channel.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information caused by Incorrect Data Forwarding during Transient Execution
children.description:
A processor event or prediction may allow incorrect or stale data to
		  be forwarded to transient operations, potentially exposing data over a
		  covert channel.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information caused by Shared Microarchitectural Predictor State that Influences Transient Execution
children.description:
Shared microarchitectural predictor state may allow code to influence
				transient execution across a hardware boundary, potentially exposing
				data that is accessible beyond the boundary over a covert channel.

children.name: Sensitive Information Exposure
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to sensitive information exposure.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
children.description: The product exposes sensitive information to an actor that is not explicitly authorized to have access to that information.

children.name: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
children.description: The code transmits data to another actor, but a portion of the data includes sensitive information that should not be accessible to that actor.

children.name: Observable Discrepancy
children.description: The product behaves differently or sends different responses under different circumstances in a way that is observable to an unauthorized actor, which exposes security-relevant information about the state of the product, such as whether a particular operation was successful or not.

children.name: Observable Response Discrepancy
children.description: The product provides different responses to incoming requests in a way that reveals internal state information to an unauthorized actor outside of the intended control sphere.

children.name: Observable Behavioral Discrepancy
children.description: The product's behaviors indicate important differences that may be observed by unauthorized actors in a way that reveals (1) its internal state or decision process, or (2) differences from other products with equivalent functionality.

children.name: Observable Internal Behavioral Discrepancy
children.description: The product performs multiple behaviors that are combined to produce a single result, but the individual behaviors are observable separately in a way that allows attackers to reveal internal state or internal decision points.

children.name: Observable Behavioral Discrepancy With Equivalent Products
children.description: The product operates in an environment in which its existence or specific identity should not be known, but it behaves differently than other products with equivalent functionality, in a way that is observable to an attacker.

children.name: Observable Timing Discrepancy
children.description: Two separate operations in a product require different amounts of time to complete, in a way that is observable to an actor and reveals security-relevant information about the state of the product, such as whether a particular operation was successful or not.

children.name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The product generates an error message that includes sensitive information about its environment, users, or associated data.

children.name: Self-generated Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The product identifies an error condition and creates its own diagnostic or error messages that contain sensitive information.

children.name: Externally-Generated Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The product performs an operation that triggers an external diagnostic or error message that is not directly generated or controlled by the product, such as an error generated by the programming language interpreter that a software application uses. The error can contain sensitive system information

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information Due to Incompatible Policies
children.description: The product's intended functionality exposes information to certain actors in accordance with the developer's security policy, but this information is regarded as sensitive according to the intended security policies of other stakeholders such as the product's administrator, users, or others whose i

children.name: Invocation of Process Using Visible Sensitive Information
children.description: A process is invoked with sensitive command-line arguments, environment variables, or other elements that can be seen by other processes on the operating system.

children.name: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Debugging Code
children.description: The product inserts sensitive information into debugging code, which could expose this information if the debugging code is not disabled in production.

children.name: Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
children.description: The product does not properly prevent a person's private, personal information from being accessed by actors who either (1) are not explicitly authorized to access the information or (2) do not have the implicit consent of the person about whom the information is collected.

children.name: Exposure of Sensitive System Information to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
children.description: The product does not properly prevent sensitive system-level information from being accessed by unauthorized actors who do not have the same level of access to the underlying system as the product does.

children.name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in an Environment Variable
children.description: The product uses an environment variable to store unencrypted sensitive information.

children.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in Test Code
children.description: Accessible test applications can pose a variety of security risks. Since developers or administrators rarely consider that someone besides themselves would even know about the existence of these applications, it is common for them to contain sensitive information or functions.

children.name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
children.description: The product writes sensitive information to a log file.

children.name: Exposure of Information Through Shell Error Message
children.description: A command shell error message indicates that there exists an unhandled exception in the web application code. In many cases, an attacker can leverage the conditions that cause these errors in order to gain unauthorized access to the system.

children.name: Servlet Runtime Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: A servlet error message indicates that there exists an unhandled exception in the web application code and may provide useful information to an attacker.

children.name: Java Runtime Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: In many cases, an attacker can leverage the conditions that cause unhandled exception errors in order to gain unauthorized access to the system.

children.name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Externally-Accessible File or Directory
children.description: The product places sensitive information into files or directories that are accessible to actors who are allowed to have access to the files, but not to the sensitive information.

children.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in Source Code
children.description: Source code on a web server or repository often contains sensitive information and should generally not be accessible to users.

children.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in an Include File
children.description: If an include file source is accessible, the file can contain usernames and passwords, as well as sensitive information pertaining to the application and system.

children.name: Exposure of Information Through Directory Listing
children.description: The product inappropriately exposes a directory listing with an index of all the resources located inside of the directory.

children.name: Server-generated Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: Certain conditions, such as network failure, will cause a server error message to be displayed.

children.name: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings
children.description: The web application uses the HTTP GET method to process a request and includes sensitive information in the query string of that request.

children.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in Source Code Comments
children.description: While adding general comments is very useful, some programmers tend to leave important data, such as: filenames related to the web application, old links or links which were not meant to be browsed by users, old code fragments, etc.

children.name: Exposure of WSDL File Containing Sensitive Information
children.description: The Web services architecture may require exposing a Web Service Definition Language (WSDL) file that contains information on the publicly accessible services and how callers of these services should interact with them (e.g. what parameters they expect and what types they return).

children.name: Incorrect Comparison Logic Granularity
children.description: The product's comparison logic is performed over a series of steps rather than across the entire string in one operation. If there is a comparison logic failure on one of these steps, the operation may be vulnerable to a timing attack that can result in the interception of the process for nefarious

children.name: Comparison Logic is Vulnerable to Power Side-Channel Attacks
children.description: A device's real time power consumption may be monitored during security token evaluation and the information gleaned may be used to determine the value of the reference token.

children.name: Device Unlock Credential Sharing
children.description: The credentials necessary for unlocking a device are shared across multiple parties and may expose sensitive information.

children.name: Debug Messages Revealing Unnecessary Information
children.description: The product fails to adequately prevent the revealing of unnecessary and potentially sensitive system information within debugging messages.

children.name: Improper Protection of Physical Side Channels
children.description:
The device does not contain sufficient protection
	mechanisms to prevent physical side channels from exposing
	sensitive information due to patterns in physically observable
	phenomena such as variations in power consumption,
	electromagnetic emissions (EME), or acoustic emissions.

children.name: Driving Intermediate Cryptographic State/Results to Hardware Module Outputs
children.description:
The product uses a hardware module implementing a cryptographic
		  algorithm that writes sensitive information about the intermediate
		  state or results of its cryptographic operations via one of its output
		  wires (typically the output port containing the final result).

children.name: Violation of Secure Design Principles
children.summary: Weaknesses in this category are related to violation of secure design principles.

children.name: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
children.description: The product performs an operation at a privilege level that is higher than the minimum level required, which creates new weaknesses or amplifies the consequences of other weaknesses.

children.name: Improper Protection of Alternate Path
children.description: The product does not sufficiently protect all possible paths that a user can take to access restricted functionality or resources.

children.name: Unimplemented or Unsupported Feature in UI
children.description: A UI function for a security feature appears to be supported and gives feedback to the user that suggests that it is supported, but the underlying functionality is not implemented.

children.name: Not Failing Securely ('Failing Open')
children.description: When the product encounters an error condition or failure, its design requires it to fall back to a state that is less secure than other options that are available, such as selecting the weakest encryption algorithm or using the most permissive access control restrictions.

children.name: Unnecessary Complexity in Protection Mechanism (Not Using 'Economy of Mechanism')
children.description: The product uses a more complex mechanism than necessary, which could lead to resultant weaknesses when the mechanism is not correctly understood, modeled, configured, implemented, or used.

children.name: Not Using Complete Mediation
children.description: The product does not perform access checks on a resource every time the resource is accessed by an entity, which can create resultant weaknesses if that entity's rights or privileges change over time.

children.name: Improper Isolation or Compartmentalization
children.description: The product does not properly compartmentalize or isolate functionality, processes, or resources that require different privilege levels, rights, or permissions.

children.name: Reliance on a Single Factor in a Security Decision
children.description: A protection mechanism relies exclusively, or to a large extent, on the evaluation of a single condition or the integrity of a single object or entity in order to make a decision about granting access to restricted resources or functionality.

children.name: Insufficient Psychological Acceptability
children.description: The product has a protection mechanism that is too difficult or inconvenient to use, encouraging non-malicious users to disable or bypass the mechanism, whether by accident or on purpose.

children.name: Reliance on Security Through Obscurity
children.description: The product uses a protection mechanism whose strength depends heavily on its obscurity, such that knowledge of its algorithms or key data is sufficient to defeat the mechanism.

children.name: Violation of Secure Design Principles
children.description: The product violates well-established principles for secure design.

children.name: Lack of Administrator Control over Security
children.description: The product uses security features in a way that prevents the product's administrator from tailoring security settings to reflect the environment in which the product is being used. This introduces resultant weaknesses or prevents it from operating at a level of security that is desired by the admin

children.name: Improper Isolation of Shared Resources on System-on-a-Chip (SoC)
children.description: The System-On-a-Chip (SoC) does not properly isolate shared resources between trusted and untrusted agents.

children.name: Improper Identifier for IP Block used in System-On-Chip (SOC)
children.description: The System-on-Chip (SoC) does not have unique, immutable identifiers for each of its components.

children.name: Non-Transparent Sharing of Microarchitectural Resources
children.description: Hardware structures shared across execution contexts (e.g., caches and branch predictors) can violate the expected architecture isolation between contexts.

children.name: Improper Isolation of Shared Resources in Network On Chip (NoC)
children.description: The Network On Chip (NoC) does not isolate or incorrectly isolates its on-chip-fabric and internal resources such that they are shared between trusted and untrusted agents, creating timing channels.

children.name: Dependency on Vulnerable Third-Party Component
children.description: The product has a dependency on a third-party component that contains one or more known vulnerabilities.
